blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ IV
TOPOLOGIES AND SHEAVESby M. Demazure (*)
(*) This text develops the substance of two oral talks by A. Grothendieck, completing them on several important points which had been passed over in silence or barely touched upon.

This exposé is intended to acquaint the reader with the essentials of the language of topologies and sheaves (without cohomology), which is particularly convenient in questions of passage to the quotient (among others).

The first three sections develop the language of passage to the quotient. The fourth, which is the central part, is an account of the theory of sheaves, oriented mainly towards applications to questions of quotients; the fifth is an application to passage to the quotient in groups and to principal homogeneous bundles. The last section deals more specifically with the category of schemes, and defines various useful topologies on this category.

The reader will find it useful to refer to [AS], [MA], [D], and SGA 4; [D] especially as regards the applications of topologies to descent theory, and SGA 4 for questions of universes (which are treated particularly badly in this exposé).
1. Universal effective epimorphisms
In the remainder of this exposé, we suppose a category \(\mathscr{C}\) has been fixed.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.1

A morphism \(u : T \to S\) is called an epimorphism if, for every object X, the corresponding map \[ X(S) = \mathrm{Hom}(S,X) \longrightarrow X(T) = \mathrm{Hom}(T,X) \] is injective. We say that \(u\) is a universal epimorphism if for every morphism \(S' \to S\), the fiber product \(T' = T \times_S S'\) exists, and \(u' : T' \to S'\) is an epimorphism.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.2

A diagram \[ \xymatrix{ A \ar[r]^-{u} & B \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^-{v_1} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_-{v_2} & C } \] of maps of sets is said to be exact if \(u\) is injective and if its image consists of the elements \(b\) of B such that \(v_1(b) = v_2(b)\). A diagram of the same type in \(\mathscr{C}\) is said to be exact if for every object X of \(\mathscr{C}\), the corresponding diagram of sets \[ \xymatrix{ A(X) \ar[r] & B(X) \ar@<0.6ex>[r] \ar@<-0.6ex>[r] & C(X) } \] is exact; we then also say that \(u\) makes A a kernel of the pair of arrows \((v_1,v_2)\). Dually, a diagram \[ \xymatrix{ C \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^-{v_1} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_-{v_2} & B \ar[r]^-{u} & A } \] in \(\mathscr{C}\) is said to be exact if it is exact as a diagram in the opposite category \(\mathscr{C}^{\circ}\), i.e. if for every object X of \(\mathscr{C}\), the corresponding diagram of sets \[ \xymatrix{ X(A) \ar[r] & X(B) \ar@<0.6ex>[r] \ar@<-0.6ex>[r] & X(C) } \] is exact. We also say that \(u\) makes A a cokernel of the pair of arrows \((v_1,v_2)\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.3

A morphism \(u : T \to S\) is called an effective epimorphism if the fibered square \(T \times_S T\) exists, and if the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ T\underset{S}{\times}T \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^-{\mathrm{pr}_1} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_-{\mathrm{pr}_2} & T \ar[r]^-{u} & S } \] is exact, i.e. if \(u\) makes S a cokernel of \((\mathrm{pr}_1,\mathrm{pr}_2)\). We say that \(u\) is a universal effective epimorphism if for every morphism \(S' \to S\), the fiber product \(T' = T \times_S S'\) exists, and the morphism \(u' : T' \to S'\) is an effective epimorphism.

We evidently have the implications: \[ \xymatrix{ \text{universal effective epim.} \ar@{=>}[r] \ar@{=>}[d] & \text{effective epim.} \ar@{=>}[d] \\ \text{universal epim.} \ar@{=>}[r] & \text{epimorphism} } \] but in general no other implication is valid.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.4

Consider morphisms \(U \xrightarrow{\ v\ } T \xrightarrow{\ u\ } S\). Then

  1. a)

    \(u,v\) epimorphisms \(\Rightarrow\) \(uv\) epimorphism,

  2. b)

    \(u,v\) univ. epim. \(\Rightarrow\) \(uv\) univ. epim., and \(u\) squarable \(\Rightarrow\) \(u\) univ. epim.
Recall that a morphism \(u : T \to S\) is said to be squarable if for every morphism \(S' \to S\), the fiber product \(T \times_S S'\) exists. Lemma 1.4 is trivial from the definitions. We conclude:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.5

Let \(u : X \to Y\) and \(u' : X' \to Y'\) be universal epimorphisms such that \(Y \times Y'\) exists; then \(X \times X'\) exists and \(u \times u' : X \times X' \to Y \times Y'\) is a universal epimorphism.
Let us also note:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.6

Let \(u : X \to Y\) be a morphism in \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\); for it to be an epimorphism (resp. universal epimorphism, resp. effective epimorphism, resp. universal effective epimorphism), it suffices that the corresponding morphism in \(\mathscr{C}\) be one, and this is also necessary if we suppose that S is a squarable object of \(\mathscr{C}\), i.e. that its product with every object of \(\mathscr{C}\) exists.

Immediate proof left to the reader. The hypothesis "S squarable" is used to interpret the \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\)-morphisms from an object Y to an object Z as being the \(\mathscr{C}\)-morphisms from Y to \(Z \times S\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.7

With the notation of 1.4: \(u,v\) effective epim. and \(v\) univ. epim. \(\Rightarrow\) \(uv\) effective epim.

To see this, consider the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ S & T \ar[l]_-{u} \ar@<0.6ex>[r] \ar@<-0.6ex>[r] & T\underset{S}{\times}T \\ & U \ar[u]^-{v} \ar@<0.6ex>[r] \ar@<-0.6ex>[r] & U\underset{S}{\times}U \ar[u]_-{v\times_S v} \\ & & U\underset{T}{\times}U \ar[u] } \] Note that by hypothesis, row 1 and column 1 are exact, and that by virtue of 1.5 and 1.6, \(v \times_S v\) is an epimorphism (\(v\) being a universal epimorphism). The conclusion follows by an obvious diagram chase: if an element of X(U) has the same images in \(X(U \times_S U)\), it has a fortiori the same images in \(X(U \times_T U)\), and hence comes from an element of X(T) since column 1 is exact. Since row 1 is exact, it suffices to check that the element in question has the same images in \(X(T \times_S T)\), and since \(v \times_S v\) is an epimorphism, it suffices to check that the images in \(X(U \times_S U)\) are the same, which is indeed the case.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.8

Consider morphisms \(U \xrightarrow{\ v\ } T \xrightarrow{\ u\ } S\). Then \(u,v\) univ. effective epim. \(\Rightarrow\) \(uv\) univ. eff. epim., and \(u\) squarable \(\Rightarrow\) \(u\) univ. eff. epim.

The first implication follows at once from 1.7. For the second, we look at the diagram (of "bisimplicial" type): \[ \xymatrix{ S & T \ar[l]_-{u} \ar@<0.6ex>[r] \ar@<-0.6ex>[r] & T\underset{S}{\times}T \\ U \ar[u]^-{vu} & U\underset{S}{\times}T \ar[l] \ar[u]^-{v} \ar@<0.6ex>[r] \ar@<-0.6ex>[r] & U\underset{S}{\times}T\underset{S}{\times}T \ar[u] \\ U\underset{S}{\times}U \ar[u] & U\underset{S}{\times}U\underset{S}{\times}T \ar[l] \ar[u] \ar@<0.6ex>[r] \ar@<-0.6ex>[r] & U\underset{S}{\times}U\underset{S}{\times}T\underset{S}{\times}T \ar[u] } \] Columns 1, 2, 3 are exact by virtue of the hypothesis "\(vu\) universal effective epimorphism"; row 2 is exact, since \(U \times_S T \to U\) is an effective epimorphism (for it has a section over U), and the same holds for row 3 (same reason). An obvious diagram chase then shows that row 1 is exact, i.e. \(u\) is an effective epimorphism. Since the hypotheses made are invariant under an arbitrary base change \(S' \to S\), it follows that \(u\) is even a universal effective epimorphism.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.9

Let \(u : X \to Y\) and \(u' : X' \to Y'\) be universal effective epimorphisms such that \(Y \times Y'\) exists; then \(X \times X'\) exists and \(u \times u' : X \times X' \to Y \times Y'\) is a universal effective epimorphism.

Proof as for 1.5 by means of the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ & Y' & X' \ar[l]_-{u'} \ar[d] \\ X \ar[d]_-{u} & X\times Y' \ar[l] \ar[u] & X\times X' \ar[l]_-{\mathrm{id}\times u'} \ar[dl]^-{u\times u'} \\ Y & Y\times Y' \ar[l] & } \]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.10

Consider a squarable morphism \(u : T \to S\), and a base change morphism \(S' \to S\) which is a universal effective epimorphism. For \(u\) to be a universal effective epimorphism, it is necessary and sufficient that \(u' : T' = T \times_S S' \to S'\) be one: \[ \xymatrix{ T \ar[d]_-{u} & T' \ar[l]_-{v'} \ar[d]^-{u'} \\ S & S' \ar[l]^-{v} } \]

Only the "sufficient" part requires a proof. Now if \(u'\) is a univ. eff. epim., the same is true of \(vu'\) thanks to 1.8, and since \(vu' = uv'\), we conclude by 1.8 that \(u\) is a univ. eff. epim.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 1.11

The same reasoning shows that in 1.10 we may replace "universal effective epimorphism" by "universal epimorphism" or "universal and effective epimorphism", or simply by "epimorphism" (and in this last case, the hypothesis "\(u\) squarable" is evidently unnecessary).
In the proof of 1.8 we used the following result, which deserves to be made explicit:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.12

Let \(u : T \to S\) be a morphism which admits a section. Then \(u\) is an epimorphism, and if \(T \times_S T\) exists, it is an effective epimorphism, and a universal effective epimorphism if moreover \(u\) is squarable.

The first assertion is contained in 1.4 a), and the third will follow at once from the second, so it suffices to establish the latter. In fact we have a stronger conclusion: for every functor \(F : \mathscr{C}^{\circ} \to (\mathrm{Ens})\) (not necessarily representable), the diagram of sets \[ \xymatrix{ F(S) \ar[r] & F(T) \ar@<0.6ex>[r] \ar@<-0.6ex>[r] & F(T\underset{S}{\times}T) } \] is exact. This can be regarded as a special case of the formalism of Čech cohomology (in dimension 0!) which we content ourselves with recalling here. Suppose merely that \(T \times_S T\) exists; we then set \[ \mathrm{H}^0(T/S,F) = \mathrm{Ker}\bigl(F(T) \rightrightarrows F(T\underset{S}{\times}T)\bigr). \]

We may regard \(\mathrm{H}^0(T/S,F)\) in an obvious way as a contravariant functor in the argument T varying in \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\), every S-morphism \(T' \to T\) defining a map \[ (+) \qquad \mathrm{H}^0(T/S,F) \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}^0(T'/S,F). \] Fix T and T' in \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\). A well-known computation shows that if there exists an S-morphism from T' to T, the corresponding map (+) is in fact independent of the choice of this morphism, so that \(\mathrm{H}^0(T/S,F)\) may be regarded as a functor on the category associated to the set \(\mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}_{/S}\) preordered by the "domination" relation (T' dominates T if there exists an S-morphism from T' to T). In particular, if T and T' are isomorphic in this last category, i.e. if each dominates the other, then (+) is an isomorphism of sets. This applies in particular to the case where T' is the final object of \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\), i.e. essentially S itself; in any case T dominates T'=S, and the converse is true precisely when T/S has a section. This establishes 1.12 in the strengthened form announced.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 1.13

For various applications, the notions introduced in the present exposé, and the results stated, should be developed more generally relative to a family of morphisms \(u_i : T_i \to S\) with the same target (instead of a single morphism \(u : T \to S\)). Thus, such a family will be said to be epimorphic if for every object X of \(\mathscr{C}\), the corresponding map \[ X(S) \longrightarrow \prod_i X(T_i) \] is injective, and we likewise introduce the notion of an effective epimorphic family and the "universal" variants of these notions. We shall assume as needed, in what follows, that the results of the present exposé extend to this more general situation.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 1.14

Every morphism which is both a monomorphism and an effective epimorphism is an isomorphism. Indeed, in the notation of 1.3, the fact that \(T \to S\) is a monomorphism implies that the two morphisms \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{pr}_1,\mathrm{pr}_2 : T\underset{S}{\times}T \ar@<0.6ex>[r] \ar@<-0.6ex>[r] & T } \] are equal (and are isomorphisms). Now a diagram \[ \xymatrix{ C \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^-{v} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_-{v} & B \ar[r]^-{u} & A } \] is exact only if \(u\) is an isomorphism, as follows immediately from the definition.
2. Descent morphisms
Let us recall the following definitions:

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.1

Let \(f : S' \to S\) be a morphism such that \(S'' = S' \times_S S'\) exists, and let \(u' : X' \to S'\) be an object over S'. A gluing datum on X'/S', relative to \(f\), is an isomorphism \[ c : X''_1 \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } X''_2 \] where \(X''_i\) (\(i=1,2\)) denotes the inverse image (assumed to exist) of X'/S' under the projection \(\mathrm{pr}_i : S'' \to S'\). We say that the gluing datum \(c\) is a descent datum if it satisfies the "cocycle condition" \[ \mathrm{pr}^*_{3,1}(c) = \mathrm{pr}^*_{3,2}(c)\,\mathrm{pr}^*_{2,1}(c) \] where \(\mathrm{pr}_{i,j}\) (\(1 \leqslant j < i \leqslant 3\)) are the canonical projections from \(S''' = S' \times_S S' \times_S S'\) to S'' (N.B. we now suppose that S''' also exists), where \(\mathrm{pr}^*_{i,j}(c)\) is the inverse image of \(c\), regarded as an S'''-morphism from \(X''_j\) to \(X''_i\), and where for every integer \(k\) between 1 and 3, \(X'''_k\) denotes the inverse image (assumed to exist) of X'/S' under the projection of index \(k\), \(q_k : S''' \to S'\).

In the second part of the definition, we have thus used identifications and abuses of notation in common use, which experience shows to be harmless, but which should obviously be avoided in a rigorous account of descent theory (which must, to a certain extent, make precise and justify these common abuses of language). Such a formal account ([D]) has been written by GIRAUD (with a view to justifying and making precise SGA 1, VII, which was never written). For a detailed account of the results of faithfully flat descent, of which constant use will be made in the present Seminar, the reader may consult SGA 1, VIII.

Let \(f : S' \to S\) still be a morphism such that \(S'' = S' \times_S S'\) exists, and let X be an object over S such that \(X' = X \times_S S'\) and \(X'' = X \times_S S''\) exist; then the inverse images of X' under \(\mathrm{pr}_i\) (\(i=1,2\)) exist and are canonically isomorphic, and consequently X'/S' is equipped with a canonical gluing datum relative to \(f\). When S''' and \(X''' = X \times_S S'''\) exist, this is even a descent datum. If Y is another object over S satisfying the same conditions as X/S, then for every S-morphism \(X \to Y\), the corresponding S'-morphism \(X' \to Y'\) is "compatible with the canonical gluing data" on X',Y'. If in particular \(S' \to S\) is a squarable morphism, then \[ X \longmapsto X' = X \underset{S}{\times} S' \] is a functor from the category \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\) to the "category of objects over S' equipped with a descent datum relative to \(f\)" — a category whose definition is left to the reader, and which is a full subcategory of the "category of objects over S' equipped with a gluing datum relative to \(f\)".
This granted:

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.2

We say that a morphism \(f : S' \to S\) is a descent morphism (resp. an effective descent morphism) if \(f\) is squarable (i.e. for every \(X \to S\), the fiber product \(X \times_S S'\) exists), and if the preceding functor \(X \mapsto X' = X \times_S S'\) from the category \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\) of objects over S to the category of objects over S' equipped with a descent datum relative to \(f\) is fully faithful (resp. an equivalence of categories).

Note that the first of these two notions can be expressed using only the notion of gluing datum (hence without bringing in the triple fiber product S'''), \(f\) being a descent morphism if \(f\) is squarable and \(X \mapsto X'\) is a fully faithful functor from the category \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\) to the category of objects over S' equipped with a gluing datum relative to \(f\). When we spell out this definition, we see that it means that for two objects X, Y over S, the following diagram of sets \[ (\mathrm{x}) \qquad \xymatrix{ \mathrm{Hom}_S(X,Y) \ar[r] & \mathrm{Hom}_{S'}(X',Y') \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^-{p_1} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_-{p_2} & \mathrm{Hom}_{S''}(X'',Y'') } \] is exact, where \(p_i(u')\) denotes the inverse image of \(u' \in \mathrm{Hom}_{S'}(X',Y')\) under the projection \(\mathrm{pr}_i : S'' \to S'\), for \(i=1,2\); indeed, the kernel of the pair \((p_1,p_2)\) is by definition none other than the set of S'-morphisms \(X' \to Y'\) compatible with the canonical gluing data.

Note that, by the definition of the inverse images Y', Y'', we have canonical bijections \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{S'}(X',Y') \simeq \mathrm{Hom}_S(X',Y) \ , \qquad \mathrm{Hom}_{S''}(X'',Y'') \simeq \mathrm{Hom}_S(X'',Y), \] so that the exactness of the diagram (x) is equivalent to that of \[ (\mathrm{xx}) \qquad \xymatrix{ \mathrm{Hom}_S(X,Y) \ar[r] & \mathrm{Hom}_S(X',Y) \ar@<0.6ex>[r] \ar@<-0.6ex>[r] & \mathrm{Hom}_S(X'',Y) } \]

obtained by applying \(\mathrm{Hom}_S(-,Y)\) to the diagram in \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\): \[ (\mathrm{xxx}) \qquad \xymatrix{ X'' \ar@<0.6ex>[r] \ar@<-0.6ex>[r] & X' \ar[r] & X } \] which is deduced from \[ \xymatrix{ S'' \ar@<0.6ex>[r] \ar@<-0.6ex>[r] & S' \ar[r] & S } \] by the base change \(X \to S\). This proves, taking 1.6 into account, the first part of:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.3

Let \(f : S' \to S\) be a morphism. If it is a universal effective epimorphism, it is a descent morphism, and the converse is true if S is a squarable object of \(\mathscr{C}\) (i.e. its product with every object of \(\mathscr{C}\) exists).

It remains to prove that if \(f\) is a descent morphism, it is a universal effective epimorphism, that is, that for every morphism \(X \to S\), the diagram (xxx) is exact, i.e. for every object Z of \(\mathscr{C}\), the transform of this diagram under \(\mathrm{Hom}(-,Z)\) is an exact diagram of sets. Now by hypothesis \(Z \times S\) exists; let Y be the object of \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\) it defines; then the transform of (xxx) under \(\mathrm{Hom}(-,Z)\) is isomorphic to the transform under \(\mathrm{Hom}_S(-,Y)\), and the latter is exact by the hypothesis on \(f\).

We may therefore apply to universal effective epimorphisms the results on descent morphisms, such as the following:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.4

Let \(f : S' \to S\) be a descent morphism (for example a universal effective epimorphism). Then:

  1. a)

    For every S-morphism \(u : X \to Y\), \(u\) is an isomorphism (resp. a monom.) if and only if \(u' : X' \to Y'\) is.

  2. b)

    Let X, Y be two subobjects of S, and X' and Y' the subobjects of S' which are the inverse images of the former. For X to be dominated by Y (resp. equal to Y), it is necessary and sufficient that the same hold for X',Y'.

For a), it follows from the definition that if \(u'\) is an isomorphism in the category of objects with gluing datum, then \(u\) is an isomorphism; and one sees at once that every isomorphism of objects over S' compatible with gluing data is an isomorphism of objects with gluing datum, i.e. its inverse is also compatible with the gluing data. For b), we reduce to proving that if X' is dominated by Y', i.e. if there exists an S'-morphism X' \(\to\) Y', then the same holds for X,Y over S. Now since \(Y' \to S'\), and hence also \(Y'' \to S''\), is a monomorphism, we see that \(X' \to Y'\) is automatically compatible with the gluing data, and hence comes from an S-morphism \(X \to Y\). Note that the proof holds more generally when we have two objects X, Y over S, with \(Y \to S\) a monomorphism, and we ask whether the morphism \(X \to S\) factors through Y: it suffices that \(X' \to S'\) factor through Y'.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.5

Let \(f : S' \to S\) be a universal effective epimorphism and \(g : S \to T\) a morphism such that \(S \times_T S\) exists. Suppose that \(S'' = S' \times_S S'\) is also a fiber product of S' with itself over T, i.e. \(S' \times_S S' \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } S' \times_T S'\). Then \(g : S \to T\) is a monomorphism (and conversely, of course).

Indeed, consider the cartesian diagram \[ \xymatrix{ S'\underset{S}{\times}S' \ar[r]^-{\sim} \ar[d] & S'\underset{T}{\times}S' \ar[d]^-{f} \\ S \ar[r] & S\underset{T}{\times}S } \] where the second horizontal arrow is the diagonal morphism. By virtue of 1.9 the second vertical arrow is a universal effective epimorphism; by hypothesis the first horizontal arrow is an isomorphism, hence by virtue of 2.4 a) or b), as one prefers, so is the first arrow \(S \to S \times_T S\), which means precisely that \(g : S \to T\) is a monomorphism.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 2.6

The notions introduced in 2.1, in terms of morphisms between certain projective limits, can be spelled out in an obvious way in terms of the contravariant functors defined by the objects S, S', X' under consideration: subject to the existence of the fiber products considered in 2.1, there is a one-to-one correspondence between the gluing (resp. descent) data on X'/S' relative to \(S' \to S\), and the gluing (resp. descent) data for the corresponding objects in \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}} = \mathrm{Hom}(\mathscr{C}^{\circ}, (\mathrm{Ens}))\). This allows one, if desired, to extend these notions to the case where no existence hypothesis on fiber products in \(\mathscr{C}\) is made.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 2.7

The notions introduced in this section generalize to the case of families of morphisms. They may moreover be presented in a more intrinsic way using the notion of a sieve (4.1). For these questions, the reader is referred to [D].
3. Universal effective equivalence relations
3.1. Equivalence relations: definitions

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.1.1

A \(\mathscr{C}\)-equivalence relation in \(X \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\) is a representable subfunctor R of the functor \(X \times X\) such that for every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\), R(S) is the graph of an equivalence relation in X(S).

This definition applies in particular to the category \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\). If we regard X as an object of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\), we then see that a \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-equivalence relation in X is nothing other than a subfunctor R of \(X \times X\) such that R(S) is the graph of an equivalence relation in X(S) for every object S of \(\mathscr{C}\).

If R is a \(\mathscr{C}\)-equivalence relation in X, we denote by \(p_i\) the morphism from R to X induced by the projection \(\mathrm{pr}_i : X \times X \to X\). We thus have a diagram \[ \xymatrix{ R \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^-{p_1} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_-{p_2} & X }. \]

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.1.2

A morphism \(u : X \to Z\) is said to be compatible with R if \(up_1 = up_2\). A cokernel object of the pair \((p_1,p_2)\) is also called a quotient object of X by R and denoted X/R.

We thus have an exact diagram \[ \xymatrix{ R \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^-{p_1} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_-{p_2} & X \ar[r]^-{p} & X/R } \] and X/R represents the covariant functor \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{C}}(X/R,Z) = \{\text{morphisms from X to Z compatible with R}\}. \] If quotient objects have been chosen in \(\mathscr{C}\), the quotient X/R is unique (when it exists).

These definitions generalize at once to the case of a \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-equivalence relation in X, but it should be noted that the identification of objects of \(\mathscr{C}\) with their images in \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\) does not commute with the formation of quotients, that is, the quotient X/R of X by R in \(\mathscr{C}\) is not a priori a quotient of X by R in \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\). One should therefore beware of rashly identifying \(\mathscr{C}\) with its image in \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\) in questions involving passage to the quotient.

In what follows, we shall say simply equivalence relation for \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-equivalence relation; we shall specify, where appropriate, when \(\mathscr{C}\)-equivalence relations are meant.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.1.3

If X is an object of \(\mathscr{C}\) over S, an equivalence relation in X over S is an equivalence relation R in X such that the structural morphism \(X \to S\) is compatible with R.

The canonical monomorphism \(R \to X \times X\) then factors through the monomorphism \[ X\underset{S}{\times}X \longrightarrow X\times X \]

and defines an equivalence relation in the object \(X \to S\) of \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\). When the quotient X/R exists, it is equipped with a canonical morphism to S, and the corresponding object of \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\) is a quotient of \(X \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}_{/S}\) by the preceding equivalence relation. Conversely, if S is a squarable object of \(\mathscr{C}\) and if \(Y \to S\) is a quotient of X by this equivalence relation (in \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\)), then Y is a quotient of X by R in \(\mathscr{C}\). In any case, we shall never have to consider quotients in \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\) which are not already quotients in \(\mathscr{C}\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.1.4

If X (resp. X') is an object of \(\mathscr{C}\) equipped with an equivalence relation R (resp. R'), a morphism \[ u : X \longrightarrow X' \] is said to be compatible with R and R' if the following equivalent conditions are satisfied:

(i) for every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\), two points of X(S) congruent modulo R(S) are carried by \(u\) to two points of X'(S) congruent modulo R'(S);

(ii) there exists a morphism \(R \to R'\) (necessarily unique) making the following diagram commute \[ \xymatrix{ R \ar[r] \ar[d] & R' \ar[d] \\ X\times X \ar[r]^-{u\times u} & X'\times X' } \]

By the universal property of X/R, there then exists (when the quotients X/R and X'/R' exist) a unique morphism \(v\) making the following diagram commute \[ \xymatrix{ X \ar[r]^-{p} \ar[d]_-{u} & X/R \ar[d]^-{v} \\ X' \ar[r]_-{p'} & X'/R' } \]

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.1.5

A subobject (\(=\) a representable subfunctor) Y of X is said to be stable under the equivalence relation R if the following equivalent conditions are satisfied:

(i) For every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\), the subset Y(S) of X(S) is stable under R(S).

(ii) The two subobjects of R which are the inverse images of Y under \(p_1\) and \(p_2\) are identical.

An important special case of a stable subobject of X is the following: the quotient X/R exists and Y is the inverse image on X of a subobject of X/R.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.1.6

Let R be an equivalence relation in X and \(X' \to X\) a morphism. The equivalence relation R' in X' obtained by the cartesian diagram \[ \xymatrix{ R' \ar[r] \ar[d] & R \ar[d] \\ X'\times X' \ar[r] & X\times X } \] is called the equivalence relation in X' inverse image of the equivalence relation R in X. In particular, if X' is a subobject of X, we say that it is the equivalence relation induced in X' by R, and denote it \(R_{X'}\).

The morphism \(X' \to X\) is compatible with R' and R; hence, when the quotients exist, we have a morphism \(X'/R' \to X/R\) (3.1.4). If X' is a subobject of X, we shall see later that in certain cases one can prove that \(X'/R' \to X/R\) is a monomorphism, and hence identifies X'/R' with a subobject of X/R. When this is so, the inverse image of this subobject in X will be a subobject of X dominating X' and stable under R: the saturation of X' for the equivalence relation R.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.1.7

If the subobject Y of X is stable under R, we have two cartesian squares, for \(i=1,2\): \[ \xymatrix{ R_Y \ar[r] \ar[d]_-{p_i} & R \ar[d]^-{p_i} \\ Y \ar[r] & X } \]

Immediate proof.
3.2. Equivalence relation defined by a group acting freely

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.2.1

Let X be an object of \(\mathscr{C}\) and H a \(\mathscr{C}\)-group acting on X (that is, equipped with a morphism of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups \[ \mathrm{H} \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}(X)\ ). \] We say that H acts freely on X if the following equivalent conditions are satisfied:

(i) For every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\), the group H(S) acts freely on X(S);

(ii) The morphism of functors \[ H\times X \longrightarrow X\times X \] defined set-theoretically by \((h,x) \mapsto (hx,x)\) is a monomorphism.

(In the preceding definition, we assumed that the group H acted "on the left" on X. There is evidently an analogous notion in the case where the group acts "on the right", that is, when we are given a group morphism from the group \(\mathrm{H}^{\circ}\) opposite to H to \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}(X)\)).

If H acts freely on X, the image of \(H \times X\) under the morphism of (ii) is an equivalence relation in X, called the equivalence relation defined by the action of H on X. When the quotient of X by this equivalence relation exists, it is denoted \(\mathrm{H}\backslash \mathrm{X}\) (X/H when H acts on the right). It represents the following covariant functor: if Z is an object of \(\mathscr{C}\), we have \[ \mathrm{Hom}(\mathrm{H}\backslash \mathrm{X},Z) = \{\text{morphisms from X to Z invariant under H}\} \] where the morphism \(f : X \to Z\) is said to be invariant under H if for every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\), the corresponding morphism \(X(S) \to Z(S)\) is invariant under the group H(S).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.2.2

Under the conditions of 3.2.1, let Y be a subobject of X. The following conditions are equivalent:

(i) Y is stable under the equivalence relation defined by H (3.1.5);

(ii) For every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\), the subset Y(S) of X(S) is stable under H(S);

(iii) There exists a morphism \(f\), necessarily unique, making the following diagram commute \[ \xymatrix{ H\times Y \ar[r]^-{f} \ar[d] & Y \ar[d] \\ H\times X \ar[r] & X } \]

Under these conditions, \(f\) defines a morphism of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups \[ H \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}(Y) \] and the equivalence relation defined in Y by this action of H is none other than the equivalence relation induced in Y by the equivalence relation defined in X by the action of H.

Immediate proof. There is evidently an analogous statement for an "action on the right". The action of H on Y defined above will be called the action induced in Y by the given action of H on X.

Now consider the following situation: H and G are two \(\mathscr{C}\)-groups and we are given a group morphism \[ u : H \longrightarrow G. \] Then H acts on G by translations (set-theoretically we put \(hg = u(h)g\)) and acts freely on it if and only if \(u\) is a monomorphism. The quotient of G by this action of H is denoted, when it exists, \(\mathrm{H}\backslash \mathrm{G}\). One likewise defines a right action of H on G and a quotient G/H. These quotients are functorial with respect to the groups involved; more precisely, we have the following lemma, stated for right quotients:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.2.3

Let \(u : H \to G\) and \(u' : H' \to G'\) be two monomorphisms of \(\mathscr{C}\)-groups. Suppose given a morphism of \(\mathscr{C}\)-groups \[ f : G \longrightarrow G'. \] The following conditions are equivalent:

(i) \(f\) is compatible with the equivalence relations defined in G and G' by H and H'.

(ii) For every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\), we have \(fu(\mathrm{H}(\mathrm{S})) \subset u'(\mathrm{H}'(\mathrm{S}))\).

(iii) There exists a morphism \(g : H \to H'\), necessarily unique and multiplicative, such that the following diagram is commutative \[ \xymatrix{ H \ar[r]^-{g} \ar[d]_-{u} & H' \ar[d]^-{u'} \\ G \ar[r]_-{f} & G' } \]

Under these conditions, if the quotients G/H and G'/H' exist, there exists a unique morphism \(\bar f\) making the following diagram commute \[ \xymatrix{ G \ar[r]^-{f} \ar[d]_-{p} & G' \ar[d]^-{p'} \\ G/H \ar[r]_-{\bar f} & G'/H' } \]

The first part is proved by reduction to the set-theoretic case. The second follows immediately from (i).

One could translate into the present situation the notions introduced above for general equivalence relations. Let us simply point out the following lemma, whose proof is immediate by reduction to the set-theoretic case:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.2.4

Let \(u : H \to G\) be a monomorphism of \(\mathscr{C}\)-groups and G' a sub-\(\mathscr{C}\)-group of G. For the subobject G' of G to be stable under the equivalence relation defined by H, it is necessary and sufficient that \(u\) factor through the canonical monomorphism \(G' \to G\), and under this condition the action of H on G' induced by the action of H on G defined by \(u\) is none other than the action deduced from the monomorphism \(H \to G'\) factoring \(u\).
3.3. Universal effective equivalence relations

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.3.1

Let \(f : X \to Y\) be a morphism. The equivalence relation defined by \(f\) in X, denoted \(\mathscr{R}(f)\), is the \(\mathscr{C}\)-equivalence relation in X which is the image of the canonical monomorphism \[ X\underset{Y}{\times}X \to X\times X. \]

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.3.2

Let R be an equivalence relation in X. We say that R is effective if

(i) R is representable (i.e. is a \(\mathscr{C}\)-equivalence relation);

(ii) the quotient \(Y = X/R\) exists in \(\mathscr{C}\);

(iii) the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ R \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^-{p_1} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_-{p_2} & X \ar[r]^-{p} & Y } \] makes R the fibered square of X over Y, that is, R is the equivalence relation defined by \(p\).

If R is an effective equivalence relation in X, then \(p\) is an effective epimorphism (1.3). If \(f : X \to Y\) is an effective epimorphism, then \(\mathscr{R}(f)\) is an effective equivalence relation in X of which Y is a quotient. There is thus a one-to-one "Galois" correspondence between effective equivalence relations in X and effective quotients of X (i.e. equivalence classes of effective epimorphisms with source X).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.3.3

We say that the equivalence relation R in X is universal effective if the quotient \(Y = X/R\) exists, and if, for every \(Y' \to Y\), the fiber products \(X' = X \times_Y Y'\) and \(R' = R \times_Y Y'\) exist and R' is a fibered square of X' over Y'.

This amounts to saying that R is effective and that \(p : X \to X/R\) is a universal effective epimorphism.

There is thus, as above, a one-to-one correspondence between universal effective equivalence relations in X and universal effective quotients of X.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.3.4

Let R be a universal effective equivalence relation in X. Let \(f : X \to Z\) be a morphism compatible with R, hence factoring through \(g : X/R \to Z\). The following conditions are equivalent:

(i) \(g\) is a monomorphism;

(ii) R is the equivalence relation defined by \(f\).

Indeed, (i) implies (ii) trivially; the converse is none other than 2.5.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.3.5

Let H be a \(\mathscr{C}\)-group acting freely on X. We say that H acts effectively, or that the action of H on X is effective (resp. universal effective), if the equivalence relation defined in X by the action of H is effective (resp. universal effective).
3.4. (M)-effectivity

In practice, it is most often difficult to characterize universal effective epimorphisms. Nevertheless, one often has at one's disposal a certain number of morphisms of this type, for example, in the theory of schemes, the faithfully flat quasi-compact morphisms. This leads to the developments below.
Let us first state a certain number of conditions bearing on a family (M) of morphisms of \(\mathscr{C}\):
(a) (M) is stable under base extension: every \(u : T \to S\) belonging to (M) is squarable, and for every \(S' \to S\), \(u' : T \times_S S' \to S'\) belongs to (M).
(b) The composite of two elements of (M) is in (M).
(c) Every isomorphism belongs to (M).
(d) Every element of (M) is an effective epimorphism.
Note that (a) and (b) imply:

(a') The cartesian product of two elements of (M) is in (M): Let \(u : X \to Y\) and \(u' : X' \to Y'\) be two S-morphisms belonging to (M). If \(Y \times_S Y'\) exists, then \(X \times_S X'\) exists and \(u \times_S u'\) belongs to (M).

This follows from the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ & Y' & X' \ar[l]_-{u'} \ar[d] \\ X \ar[d]_-{u} & X\underset{S}{\times}Y' \ar[l] \ar[u] & X\underset{S}{\times}X' \ar[l] \ar[dl]^-{u\times_S u'} \\ Y & Y\underset{S}{\times}Y' \ar[l] & } \]
Likewise (a) and (d) imply:
(d') Every element of (M) is a universal effective epimorphism.

The family \((\mathrm{M}_0)\) of universal effective epimorphisms satisfies conditions (a) to (d) of 3.4.1. Indeed, (a), (c) and (d) hold by definition, and (b) follows from 1.8. In what follows, we shall suppose given a family (M) of morphisms of \(\mathscr{C}\) satisfying these conditions: our results will therefore apply in particular to the family \((\mathrm{M}_0)\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.4.3

We say that the equivalence relation R in X is of type (M) if it is representable and if \(p_1 \in (\mathrm{M})\) (which by (b) and (c) implies that \(p_2 \in (\mathrm{M})\)). We say that R is (M)-effective if it is effective and if the canonical morphism \(X \to X/R\) belongs to (M). We say that the quotient Y of X is (M)-effective if the canonical morphism \(X \to Y\) belongs to (M).

From this definition the following consequences result:

(i) An (M)-effective equivalence relation is universal effective and of type (M). This follows respectively from (d), and from (a) by the cartesian diagram \[ \xymatrix{ R \ar[r]^-{p_2} \ar[d]_-{p_1} & X \ar[d]^-{p} \\ X \ar[r]_-{p} & X/R } \]

(ii) An (M)-effective quotient is universal effective.

(iii) The maps \(R \mapsto X/R\) and \(p \mapsto \mathscr{R}(p)\) realize a one-to-one correspondence between (M)-effective equivalence relations in X and (M)-effective quotients of X.

(iv) \((\mathrm{M}_0)\)-effective is equivalent to universal effective.

Let H be an S-group whose structural morphism belongs to (M). Then if H acts freely on the S-object X, it defines in it an equivalence relation of type (M). Indeed, by (a) the fiber product \(H \times_S X\) exists and \(\mathrm{pr}_2 : H \times_S X \to X\) belongs to (M). We say that the action of H is (M)-effective if the equivalence relation defined in X by this action is (M)-effective.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.4.5 ((M)-effectivity and base change)

Let R be an (M)-effective equivalence relation in X over S. Put \(Y = X/R\). Let \(S' \to S\) be a base change such that \(Y' = Y \times_S S'\) exists. Then \(X' = X \times_S S'\) exists, \(R' = R \times_S S'\) is an (M)-effective equivalence relation in X' over S', and \(X'/R' \simeq (X/R)'\).

Indeed, the canonical morphisms \(X \to Y\) and \(R \to Y\) belong to (M), so by (a') X' and R' are representable. By associativity of the product, R' is the equivalence relation defined in X' by the canonical morphism \(X' \to Y'\), which belongs to (M), whence the conclusion.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.4.6 ((M)-effectivity and cartesian products)

Let R (resp. R') be an (M)-effective equivalence relation in X (resp. X') over S. If \((X/R) \times_S (X'/R')\) exists, then \(X \times_S X'\) exists, \(R \times_S R'\) is an (M)-effective equivalence relation in \(X \times_S X'\) over S, and \[ (X\underset{S}{\times}X')/(R\underset{S}{\times}R') \simeq X/R \underset{S}{\times} X'/R'. \]

Put \(Y = X/R, Y' = X'/R'\). By (a'), the fiber product \(X \times_S X'\) exists and the canonical morphism \[ q : X\underset{S}{\times}X' \longrightarrow Y\underset{S}{\times}Y' \] belongs to (M). Now the formula \[ (X\times X')\underset{(Y\times Y')}{\times}(X\times X') \simeq (X\underset{Y}{\times}X)\times(X'\underset{Y'}{\times}X') \] (all products not otherwise indicated are taken over S) shows that \(R \times_S R'\) is the equivalence relation defined by \(q\) in \(X \times_S X'\), which completes the proof.
3.5. Construction of quotients by descent

It frequently happens that one does not know how to construct a quotient directly, but that one knows how to do so after a suitable base change. The present section gives a useful criterion in this situation.
We saw in section 2 the definition of a descent datum on an object X' over S' relative to a morphism \(S' \to S\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.5.1

We say that a descent datum on X' relative to \(S' \to S\) is effective if X' equipped with this descent datum is isomorphic to the inverse image on S' of an object X over S, equipped with its canonical descent datum.

If \(S' \to S\) is a descent morphism (2.2), then the X of the definition is unique up to unique isomorphism. To say that \(S' \to S\) is an effective descent morphism (2.2) is to say that it is a descent morphism and that every descent datum relative to this morphism is effective.

Now consider an equivalence relation R in an object X over S. Let X' (resp. X'', resp. X''') be the inverse images of X on S', \(S'' = S' \times_S S'\) and \(S''' = S' \times_S S' \times_S S'\), and let R', R'', R''' be the equivalence relations deduced from R by inverse image. Suppose that the equivalence relation R' in X' is (M)-effective, and consider the quotient \(Y' = X'/R'\), which is an object over S'. Its two inverse images on S'' are isomorphic to X''/R'' by 3.4.5. The S'-object Y' is therefore equipped with a canonical gluing datum. Using likewise the uniqueness of X'''/R''', one sees that it is even a descent datum. (Remark: we have implicitly assumed in this proof that all the fiber products written down exist, which is the case in particular if \(S' \to S\) is squarable, for example a descent morphism).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.5.2

Let R be an equivalence relation in the object X over S. Let \(S' \to S\) be a universal effective epimorphism. Suppose that every S-morphism whose inverse image on S' is in (M) is itself in (M). The following conditions are equivalent:

(i) R is (M)-effective in X;

(ii) R' is (M)-effective in X' and the canonical descent datum on X'/R' is effective.

If this is so, the "descended" object of X'/R' is canonically isomorphic to X/R.

The fact that (i) implies (ii) is none other than the translation into the language of descent of 3.4.4. If we prove the converse, the last assertion of the proposition will be a consequence of the fact that a universal effective epimorphism is a descent morphism (2.3), and hence that the "descended object" is unique (\(=\) up to unique isomorphism).

Let us therefore prove (ii) \(\Rightarrow\) (i). Let Y' be the quotient X'/R' and Y the descended object. Since the canonical morphism \(p' : X' \to X'/R' = Y'\) is by construction compatible with the descent data (its inverse images on S'' coincide with the canonical morphism \(X'' \to X''/R''\)), it comes by inverse image on S' from an S-morphism \(p : X \to Y\). Since \(p'\) belongs to (M), it follows from the hypothesis made on the morphism \(S' \to S\) that \(p\) also belongs to (M). Since \(p'\) is compatible with the equivalence relation R', \(p\) is compatible with R, again because a universal effective epimorphism is a descent morphism. We thus have a morphism \[ R \longrightarrow X\underset{Y}{\times}X. \]

To prove that R is (M)-effective and that Y is isomorphic to X/R, it suffices to prove that this morphism is an isomorphism. Now it becomes one after extension of the base from S to S', since R' is effective; it is therefore an isomorphism for the same reason as before (2.4).

Note that the hypothesis in the statement is satisfied if we take for (M) the family \((\mathrm{M}_0)\) of universal effective epimorphisms and if \(\mathscr{C}\) has fiber products (1.10). We deduce:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.5.3

Suppose that \(\mathscr{C}\) has fiber products (over S would suffice). Let R be an equivalence relation in X over S and \(S' \to S\) a universal effective epimorphism. The following conditions are equivalent:

(i) R is universal effective in X,

(ii) R' is universal effective in X' and the canonical descent datum on X'/R' is effective.

If this is so, the "descended" object of X'/R' is canonically isomorphic to X/R.
4. Topologies and sheaves

The notion of a sieve, and the presentation of the notion of a topology (4.2.1) adopted here (more intrinsic and more convenient in many respects than that by covering families of [MA]), are due to J. GIRAUD [AS].
4.1. Sieves

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.1.1

A sieve of the category \(\mathscr{C}\) is a subfunctor C of the final functor \(\underline{e} : \mathscr{C}^{\circ} \to (\mathrm{Ens})\).

To every sieve C of \(\mathscr{C}\) we associate the set E(C) of objects X of \(\mathscr{C}\) such that \(C(X) \neq \varnothing\), that is, such that the structural morphism \(X \to \underline{e}\) factors through C. We thus have the equivalences \[ (+) \qquad \begin{cases} X \in E(C) \iff C(X) = \underline{e}(X) = \{\varnothing\}. \\ X \notin E(C) \iff C(X) = \varnothing. \end{cases} \] The set E(C) enjoys the following property:

\[ (++) \qquad \text{If } X \in E(C) \text{ and if } \mathrm{Hom}(Y,X) \neq \varnothing, \text{ then } Y \in E(C). \]

Conversely, if E is a subset of \(\mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\) enjoying property (++), then E can be written in a unique way in the form E(C), where C is defined by the formulas (+). There is thus a one-to-one correspondence between the sieves of \(\mathscr{C}\) and the subsets of \(\mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\) satisfying condition (++). By abuse of language, we shall sometimes say that the set E(C) is a sieve of \(\mathscr{C}\).

If we equip the set \(\mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\) with its natural preorder structure (Y dominating X if there exists an arrow from Y to X), the sets E(C) are thus the subsets of \(\mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\) which contain every object dominating one of their elements. The set of sieves of \(\mathscr{C}\) carries an order structure; we say that C is finer than C' if \(C \subset C'\), or, what comes to the same thing, \(E(C) \subset E(C')\). We have \(E(C) \cap E(C') = E(C \times C')\) and the set of sieves of \(\mathscr{C}\) is filtered.

Every subset E of \(\mathrm{Ob}\,\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\), for example a subset of \(\mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\), defines a sieve C(E): the set of \(X \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\) such that \(F(X) \neq \varnothing\) for at least one \(F \in E\) satisfies condition (++) and defines the desired sieve.

This sieve may also be defined as the image of the family of morphisms \(\{F \to \underline{e}, F \in E\}\) in the sense of the following definition:

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.1.2

Let \(\{F_i \to F\}\) be a family of morphisms of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\) with the same target F. The image of this family is the subfunctor of F defined by \[ S \rightsquigarrow \bigcup_i \mathrm{Im}\,F_i(S) \subset F(S). \]

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.1.3

The formation of the image commutes with base extension: in the preceding notation, denote by I the image of the family \(\{F_i \to F\}\); for every morphism \(G \to F\) of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\), the image of the family of morphisms \(\{F_i \times_F G \to G\}\) is the subfunctor \(I \times_F G\) of G.

If C is a sieve of \(\mathscr{C}\) and E a subset of \(\mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\) such that \(C(E) = C\), we say that E is a base of C. Every sieve C has a base, for example the set E(C). We propose to describe the set Hom(C,F), where C is a sieve of \(\mathscr{C}\) and F an object of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\), using a base of C.

Let \(\{S_i\}\) be a base of the sieve C; for each pair \((i,j)\), we have a diagram \[ \xymatrix{ S_i\times S_j \ar[r] \ar[d] & S_i \ar[d] \\ S_j \ar[r] & \underline{e} } \] whence a diagram of sets \[ \xymatrix{ \Gamma(F) = \mathrm{Hom}(\underline{e},F) \ar[r]^-{i} & \prod_i \mathrm{Hom}(S_i,F) \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^-{j_1} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_-{j_2} & \prod_{i,j} \mathrm{Hom}(S_i\times S_j,F) } \] such that \(j_1 i = j_2 i\). We thus have a morphism \[ \mathrm{Hom}(\underline{e},F) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ker}\left(\prod_i \mathrm{Hom}(S_i,F) \rightrightarrows \prod_{i,j}\mathrm{Hom}(S_i\times S_j,F)\right). \] One checks immediately:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.1.3

We have an isomorphism, functorial in F, \[ \mathrm{Hom}(C,F) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Ker}\left(\prod_i \mathrm{Hom}(S_i,F) \rightrightarrows \prod_{i,j}\mathrm{Hom}(S_i\times S_j,F)\right), \] such that the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{Hom}(\underline{e},F) \ar[r] \ar@{=}[d] & \mathrm{Ker}\left(\prod_i \mathrm{Hom}(S_i,F) \rightrightarrows \prod_{i,j}\mathrm{Hom}(S_i\times S_j,F)\right) \ar[d]^-{\wr} \\ \mathrm{Hom}(\underline{e},F) \ar[r] & \mathrm{Hom}(C,F) } \] where the last row is induced by the canonical morphism \(C \to \underline{e}\), is commutative.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.1.4

Suppose that the fiber products \(S_i \times S_j\) are representable, for example that the \(S_i\) are squarable. Then for every \(F \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\) we have an isomorphism \[ \mathrm{Hom}(C,F) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Ker}\left[\prod_i F(S_i) \rightrightarrows \prod_{i,j} F(S_i\times S_j)\right]. \]

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 4.1.5

Let R be a sieve of \(\mathscr{C}\); denote by \(\bar{\mathrm{R}}\) the full subcategory of \(\mathscr{C}\) whose set of objects is E(R) and by \[ i_{\mathrm{R}} : \bar{\mathrm{R}} \longrightarrow \mathscr{C} \] the inclusion functor. We have an isomorphism, functorial in \(F \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\), \[ \mathrm{Hom}(R,F) \longrightarrow \Gamma(F \circ i_{\mathrm{R}}) \] such that the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{Hom}(\underline{e},F) \ar[r] \ar[d]_-{\wr} & \mathrm{Hom}(R,F) \ar[d]^-{\wr} \\ \Gamma(F) \ar[r] & \Gamma(F\circ i_{\mathrm{R}}) } \] where the second row is induced by the functor \(i_{\mathrm{R}}\), is commutative.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.1.6

Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be a category. A sieve of the object S of \(\mathscr{C}\) is a sieve of the category \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\).

A sieve of S is thus a sub-\(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-object of S. To it corresponds canonically a subset of \(\mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}_{/S}\) containing the source of every arrow whose target it contains. By abuse of language, such a set will also be called a sieve of S.
4.2. Topologies: definitions

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.2.1

Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be a category. A topology on \(\mathscr{C}\) is the datum, for each S of \(\mathscr{C}\), of a set J(S) of sieves of S, called covering sieves or refinements of S, this datum satisfying the following axioms:

(T 1) For every refinement R of S and every morphism \(T \to S\), the sieve \(R \times_S T\) of T is covering ("stability under base change").

(T 2) If R,C are two sieves of S, if R is covering and if for every \(T \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\) and every morphism \(T \to R\), the sieve \(C \times_S T\) of T is covering, then C is a refinement of S.

(T 3) If \(C \supset R\) are two sieves of S and if R is covering, then C is covering.

(T 4) For every S, S is a refinement of S.

These axioms may be reformulated as follows: for every object F of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\), denote by J(F) the set of subfunctors R of F such that for every morphism \(T \to F\) of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\), where T is representable, \(R \times_F T\), which is a sieve of T, is covering. By virtue of (T 1), this notation is indeed compatible with the preceding one. We shall
also say that \(R \in J(F)\) is a refinement of F. One checks immediately that the preceding axioms imply the following properties:

(T' 0) If \(F \supset G\) are two objects of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\), and if for every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\) and every morphism \(S \to F\), \(G \times_F S \in J(S)\), then \(G \in J(F)\).
(T' 1) If \(G \in J(F)\), and if \(H \to F\) is a morphism of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\), then \(G \times_F H \in J(H)\).
(T' 2) If \(F \supset G \supset H\) are three objects of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\), if \(G \in J(F)\) and \(H \in J(G)\), then \(H \in J(F)\).
(T' 3) If \(F \supset G \supset H\) are three objects of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\) and if \(H \in J(F)\), then \(G \in J(F)\).
(T' 4) For every \(F \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\), \(F \in J(F)\).

Conversely, if we are given for every \(F \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\) a set J(F) of subobjects of F satisfying properties (T' 0) to (T' 4), the map \(S \mapsto J(S)\) defines a topology on \(\mathscr{C}\), and the two preceding constructions are inverse to each other.
From (T' 1), (T' 2) and (T' 3) results the following property:
(T' 5) If G and H are two subobjects of F, and if \(G,H \in J(F)\), then \(G \cap H \in J(F)\).
The set J(H), ordered by the relation \(\supset\), is therefore filtered; this remark will serve us later.

We say that the topology defined by J is finer than the topology defined by J' if for every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\), \(J(S) \supset J'(S)\) (it comes to the same thing to say that for every \(F \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\), \(J(F) \supset J'(F)\)).

Every set of topologies on \(\mathscr{C}\) has a greatest lower bound: let I be an index set, and for each \(i \in I\), let \(S \mapsto J_i(S)\) be a topology on \(\mathscr{C}\). Put \(J(S) = \bigcap_{i \in I} J_i(S)\); it is immediate that we have thus defined a topology on \(\mathscr{C}\), and that it is indeed the greatest lower bound of the given set.

In particular, let us give ourselves for each \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\) a set E(S) of sieves of S. The topology generated by these sets is the coarsest topology for which the elements of E(S) are refinements of S for every S.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.2.3

Let \(\{F_i \to F\}\) be a family of morphisms of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\). Let \(G \subset F\) be the image (4.1.2) of this family. The family is said to be covering if \(G \in J(F)\).

A morphism is said to be covering if the family reduced to this morphism is covering.

This definition applies in particular to an inclusion: a sieve C of S is covering if and only if the canonical morphism \(C \to S\) is covering.

Axioms (T' 0) to (T' 5) imply the following properties for covering families:

(C 0) Let \(\{F_i \to F\}\) be a family of morphisms of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\). If for every representable base change \(S \to F\), the family \(\{F_i \times_F S \to S\}\) is covering, then the initial family is covering as well.

(C 1) For every covering family \(\{F_i \to F\}\) and every morphism \(G \to F\), the family \(\{F_i \times_F G \to G\}\) is covering ("stability under base change").

(C 2) If \(\{F_i \to F\}\) is a covering family and if, for each \(i\), \(\{F_{ij} \to F_i\}\) is a covering family, then the composite family \(\{F_{ij} \to F\}\) is covering ("stability under composition").

(C 3) If \(\{G_j \to F\}\) is a covering family, and if \(\{F_i \to F\}\) is a family of morphisms with target F such that for each \(j\) there exists an \(i\) such that \(G_j \to F\) factors through \(F_i \to F\), then \(\{F_i \to F\}\) is covering ("saturation").
(C 4) Every family reduced to an isomorphism is covering.
Note that (C 2) and (C 3) also imply:

(C 5) If \(\{F_i \to F\}\) is a family of morphisms with target F such that there exists a covering family \(\{G_j \to F\}\) such that for every \(j\) the family \(\{F_i \times_F G_j \to G_j\}\) is covering, then the family \(\{F_i \to F\}\) is covering ("a locally covering family is covering").

Conversely, let \(\mathscr{C}\) be a category having fiber products, and let us give ourselves for each \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\) a set of families of morphisms of \(\mathscr{C}\) with target S, said to be covering, satisfying axioms (C 1) to (C 4) (hence also (C 5), which is a consequence of them). For every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\), let J(S) be the set of sieves of S having a covering base (or, what comes to the same thing by (C 3), all of whose bases are covering). Then \(S \mapsto J(S)\) defines a topology on \(\mathscr{C}\). The two preceding constructions are inverse to each other.

In fact, in applications, it is impractical to consider all covering families, since one sometimes has rather simple descriptions of a "sufficient" number of these families. This leads to the following

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.2.5

Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be a category. A pretopology on \(\mathscr{C}\) is the datum, for each \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\), of a set R(S) of families of morphisms \(\{S_i \to S\}\) with target S, said to be covering for the pretopology in question, satisfying the following axioms:

(P 1) For every family \(\{S_i \to S\} \in R(S)\) and every morphism \(T \to S\), the fiber products \(S_i \times_S T\) exist and \(\{S_i \times_S T \to T\} \in R(T)\).

(P 2) If \(\{S_i \to S\} \in R(S)\) and if for each \(i\) \(\{T_{ij} \to S_i\} \in R(S_i)\), then the composite family \(\{T_{ij} \to S\}\) belongs to R(S).

(P 3) Every family reduced to an isomorphism is covering.

The topology generated by R is the coarsest topology for which the given families are covering.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.2.6

Let, for every S, J(S) be the set of sieves of S which are covering for the topology generated by the pretopology R. Let \(J_R(S)\) be the part of J(S) consisting of the sieves defined by the families of R(S). Then \(J_R(S)\) is cofinal in J(S): every refinement of S contains a sieve defined by a family of R(S).

Let, for every S, J'(S) be the set of sieves of S containing a sieve of \(J_R(S)\). We evidently have \(J'(S) \subset J(S)\). To show that \(J(S) = J'(S)\), it suffices to show that the J'(S) form a topology on \(\mathscr{C}\), that is, satisfy axioms (T 1) to (T 4). Now (T 1), (T 3), (T 4) are evidently satisfied. It remains to check (T 2). So let R be an element of J'(S) and C a subsieve of R; we suppose that for every \(T \to R\), \(C \times_R T\) is in J'(T), and we must prove that \(C \in J'(S)\). By the definition of J', R contains a refinement R' defined by a family \(\{S_i \to S\} \in R(S)\). Since (T 3) has been checked, it suffices to prove that \(R' \cap C \in J'(S)\), so we may assume that \(R = R'\). By hypothesis, for every \(i\), \(C \times_S S_i \in J'(S_i)\); hence for each \(i\) there exists a covering family \(\{T_{ij} \to S_i\} \in R(S_i)\) such that \(T_{ij} \to S_i\) factors through \(C \times_S S_i \to S_i\). The morphism \(T_{ij} \to S\) therefore factors through \(C \to S\), which shows that C contains the sieve defined by the composite family \(\{T_{ij} \to S\}\), and we are done by (P 2).

Axioms (P 1) to (P 3) are those of [MA]. Given the practical interest of pretopologies, we shall interpret each important result by means of a pretopology defining the given topology.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 4.2.7

One may introduce a slightly more general notion: one gives for each S a set of covering families satisfying (P 1), (P 3) and proposition 4.2.6. This occurs in particular when the given families satisfy (P 1), (P 3) and (C 5). The reader may consult [D].

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.2.8

Let S be an object of \(\mathscr{C}\). Let \(\mathscr{P}(S')\) be a relation involving an argument \(S' \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}_{/S}\). We suppose that \(\mathrm{Hom}(S'',S') \neq \varnothing\) implies \(\mathscr{P}(S') \Rightarrow \mathscr{P}(S'')\). We say that \(\mathscr{P}\) is true locally on S if the following equivalent conditions are satisfied:

(i) The set of \(S' \to S\) such that \(\mathscr{P}(S')\) is true is a refinement of S

(ii) There exists a refinement of S such that \(\mathscr{P}(S')\) is true for every S' of this refinement.

(iii) (If the given topology is defined by a pretopology). There exists a family covering for this pretopology such that \(\mathscr{P}(S')\) is true for every S' of this family.

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
Example 4.2.9

Let \(f : X \to Y\) be an S-morphism. We say that \(f\) is locally an isomorphism if there exists a covering family \(\{S_i \to S\}\) such that for every \(i\), \(f \times_S S_i\) is an isomorphism. It comes to the same thing to require that there exist a refinement R of S such that for every \(T \to R\), \(X(T) \to Y(T)\) is an isomorphism.

We shall see many other examples of "local" language in what follows.
4.3. Presheaves, sheaves, sheaf associated with a presheaf

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.3.1

Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be a category. A presheaf of sets on \(\mathscr{C}\) is any contravariant functor from \(\mathscr{C}\) to the category of sets. The category \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}} = \mathrm{Hom}(\mathscr{C}^{\circ},(\mathrm{Ens}))\) is called the category of presheaves on \(\mathscr{C}\). If \(\mathscr{C}\) is equipped with a topology, we say that the presheaf P is separated (resp. is a sheaf) if for every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\) and every \(R \in J(S)\), the canonical map \[ (+) \qquad P(S) = \mathrm{Hom}(S,P) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}(R,P) \] is injective (resp. bijective). The category of sheaves, denoted \(\widetilde{\mathscr{C}}\), is the full subcategory of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\) whose objects are the sheaves.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.3.2

Let P be a separated presheaf (resp. a sheaf). For every functor \(H \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\) and every \(R \in J(H)\), the canonical map \[ (+) \qquad \mathrm{Hom}(H,P) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}(R,P) \] is injective (resp. bijective).

Indeed, let P be a separated presheaf, H a presheaf, \(R \in J(H)\), and \(u,v : H \to P\) such that \(uj = vj\) (see diagram). For every \(f : S \to H\), \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\), \(R \times_H S\) is a refinement of S and \(ufj_S = vfj_S\). Since P is separated, we deduce \(uf = vf\). This being true for every representable S, we have \(u = v\). \[ \xymatrix{ R \ar[r]^-{j} \ar[d] & H \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^-{u} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_-{v} & P \\ R\underset{H}{\times}S \ar[r]_-{j_S} \ar[ur] & S \ar[u]_-{f} & } \qquad \xymatrix{ R\underset{H}{\times}S \ar[r]^-{f_R} \ar[d]_-{j_S} & R \ar[r]^-{g} \ar[d]^-{j} & P \\ S \ar[r]_-{f} & H \ar[uur]_-{h} & } \]

Suppose now that P is a sheaf. Let \(g : R \to P\); let us show that it factors through H. For every \(f : S \to H\), \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\), \(g \circ f_R : R \times_F S \to P\) factors in a unique way through S, hence defines a morphism \(h : S \to P\), which is evidently functorial with respect to \(f\), by uniqueness.

We have thus defined for every S a map from F(S) to P(S), functorial in S, hence a morphism from F to P which indeed satisfies the required conditions.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.3.3 ([AS], 1.3)

Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be a category. Let P be a presheaf on \(\mathscr{C}\); for every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\), denote by J(S) the set of sieves R of S such that for every \(T \to S\), the map \[ (+) \qquad \mathrm{Hom}(T,P) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}(R\underset{S}{\times}T,P) \] is injective (resp. bijective). Then the J(S) define a topology on \(\mathscr{C}\), i.e. satisfy axioms (T 1) to (T 4).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.3.4

Let, for every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\), K(S) be a family of sieves satisfying (T 1). Let P be a presheaf on \(\mathscr{C}\). For it to be separated (resp. a sheaf) for the topology generated by the K(S), it is necessary and sufficient that for every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\) and every \(R \in K(S)\), the canonical map \[ (+) \qquad \mathrm{Hom}(S,P) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}(R,P) \] be injective (resp. bijective).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.3.5

Let, for each \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\), R(S) be a set of families of morphisms of \(\mathscr{C}\) with target S, satisfying (P 1) (for example defining a pretopology). Let P be a presheaf on \(\mathscr{C}\). For P to be separated (resp. a sheaf) for the topology generated by R, it is necessary and sufficient that for every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\) and every family \(\{S_i \to S\} \in R(S)\), the map \[ \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{S}) \longrightarrow \prod_i \mathrm{P}(S_i) \] be injective (resp. the diagram \[ \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{S}) \longrightarrow \prod_i \mathrm{P}(S_i) \rightrightarrows \prod_{i,j} \mathrm{P}(S_i\underset{S}{\times}S_j) \] be exact).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.3.6

Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be a category. The canonical topology on \(\mathscr{C}\) is the finest topology for which all representable functors are sheaves.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.3.7

For a sieve R of S to be a refinement for the canonical topology, it is necessary and sufficient that for every morphism \(T \to S\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) and every \(X \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\), the canonical map \[ \mathrm{Hom}(T,X) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}(R\underset{S}{\times}T,X) \] be bijective.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.3.8

A covering sieve for the canonical topology will be called a universal effective epimorphic sieve.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.3.9

A universal effective epimorphic family defines a universal effective epimorphic sieve. Conversely, every squarable family defining a universal effective epimorphic sieve is universal effective epimorphic.

Let us now pass to the construction of the associated sheaf. Let P be a presheaf and S an object of \(\mathscr{C}\). If \(R \supset R'\) are two refinements of S, we have a diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{Hom}(S,P) \ar[r] & \mathrm{Hom}(R,P) \ar[d] \\ & \mathrm{Hom}(R',P) } \]

The ordered set J(S) is filtered, as we have already remarked. Since S is an element of J(S), we have an obvious morphism \[ \mathrm{Hom}(S,P) \longrightarrow \varinjlim_{R\in J(S)} \mathrm{Hom}(R,P). \]

We put \(\overset{\circ}{\mathrm{H}}^0(S,P) = \varinjlim_{R \in J(S)} \mathrm{Hom}(R,P)\). One checks that \(\overset{\circ}{\mathrm{H}}^0(S,P)\) depends functorially on S, hence defines a functor LP by \[ (++) \qquad \mathrm{Hom}(S,\mathrm{LP}) = \overset{\circ}{\mathrm{H}}^0(S,P) = \varinjlim_{R\in J(S)} \mathrm{Hom}(R,P). \]
By construction we have morphisms \[ \ell_{\mathrm{P}} : \mathrm{P} \longrightarrow \mathrm{LP} \] \[ z_{\mathrm{R}} : \mathrm{Hom}(R,P) \to \mathrm{Hom}(S,\mathrm{LP}). \]

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.3.10

(i) For every refinement R of S and every \(u : R \to P\), the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ P \ar[r]^-{\ell_{\mathrm{P}}} \ar[d]_-{u} & \mathrm{LP} \ar[d]^-{z_{\mathrm{R}}(u)} \\ R \ar[r]_-{i_{\mathrm{R}}} & S } \] is commutative.

(ii) For every morphism \(S \xrightarrow{\ v\ } \mathrm{LP}\), there exist a refinement \(h\) of S and a morphism \(u : R \to P\) with \(v = z_{\mathrm{R}}(u)\).

(iii) Let Q be a functor and \(u,v : Q \to P\) such that \(\ell_{\mathrm{P}}u = \ell_{\mathrm{P}}v\). Then the kernel of the pair \((u,v)\) is a refinement of Q.

(iv) Let \(u : R \to P\) and \(u' : R' \to P\); for \(z_{\mathrm{R}}(u) = z_{\mathrm{R}'}(u')\) to hold, it is necessary and sufficient that there exist a refinement \(R'' \subset R \cap R'\) of S such that \(u\) and \(u'\) coincide on \(R''\).

Proof (i): We must check that \(z_{\mathrm{R}}(u)i_{\mathrm{R}} = \ell_{\mathrm{P}}u\). For this, it suffices to check that the composites of these two morphisms with every morphism \(T \xrightarrow{\ g\ } R\), where T is representable, are equal. Now consider \(f = i_{\mathrm{R}}g\) and the fiber product \(R' = R \times_S T\). \[ \xymatrix{ P \ar[r]^-{\ell_{\mathrm{P}}} \ar[d]_-{u} & \mathrm{LP} \\ R \ar[r]^-{i_{\mathrm{R}}} & S \\ R' \ar[u]^-{p} \ar[r]_-{i_{\mathrm{R}'}}^-{\sim} & T \ar[u]_-{f} \ar[ul]_-{g} } \]

By the definition of \(\ell_{\mathrm{P}}\), \(\ell_{\mathrm{P}}ug = z_{\mathrm{R}}(u)f\) (this is the special case of what we are trying to prove in which \(i_{\mathrm{R}}\) is an isomorphism); now \(z_{\mathrm{R}}(u)f = z_{\mathrm{R}}(u)i_{\mathrm{R}}g\).

(ii) and (iv) merely translate the definition of Hom(S,LP) as an inductive limit.

(iii): If K denotes the kernel of the pair \((u,v)\), for each morphism \(f : S \to Q\) where S is representable, \(K \times_Q S\) dominates the kernel of the pair of arrows \(uf,vf : S \to P\). We are thus reduced to proving the assertion in the case where \(Q = S\) is representable. But in this case, it follows from (ii) and (iv) that K contains a refinement of S, hence is a refinement of S.

One checks finally that \(P \mapsto \mathrm{LP}\) defines a functor \[ \mathrm{L} : \widehat{\mathscr{C}} \longrightarrow \widehat{\mathscr{C}} \] and \(P \mapsto \ell_{\mathrm{P}}\) a morphism of functors \[ \ell : \mathrm{Id}_{\widehat{\mathscr{C}}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{L}. \] Let us now state the essential result:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.3.11

(i) If P is an arbitrary presheaf, LP is separated and \(\ell_{\mathrm{P}} : \mathrm{P} \to \mathrm{LP}\) is covering (4.2.3).

(ii) If P is a sheaf, \(\mathrm{P} \to \mathrm{LP}\) is an isomorphism.

(iii) For every presheaf P and every separated presheaf (resp. sheaf) F, the map \[ \mathrm{Hom}(\ell_{\mathrm{P}},F) : \mathrm{Hom}(\mathrm{LP},F) \to \mathrm{Hom}(P,F) \] is injective (resp. bijective).

(iv) If P is separated, \(\ell_{\mathrm{P}} : \mathrm{P} \to \mathrm{LP}\) is a covering monomorphism (so P is a sieve of LP), and LP is a sheaf.

Proof: (i) It is clear, first of all, that \(\mathrm{P} \to \mathrm{LP}\) is covering; indeed, for every \(S \to \mathrm{LP}\), the morphism obtained by base change \(\mathrm{P} \times_{\mathrm{LP}} S \to S\) is dominated by the inclusion morphism of a sieve of S (by 4.3.10 (i) and (ii)), hence is covering. If, on the other hand, two morphisms \(S \xrightarrow{\ v_1,v_2\ } \mathrm{LP}\) induce the same morphism from a refinement R of S, let us show that they are equal. There exist refinements \(R_i\), \(i = 1,2\), and morphisms \(u_i : R_i \to P\) such that \(z_{R_i}(u_i) = v_i\). By taking R small enough, we may assume that \(R_1 = R_2 = R\). It then follows from the commutative diagram of 4.3.10 (i) that \(\ell_{\mathrm{P}}u_1 = \ell_{\mathrm{P}}u_2\). By loc. cit. (ii), \(u_1\) and \(u_2\) therefore coincide on a refinement of R, hence a refinement of S, which implies that \(z_{\mathrm{R}}(u_1) = z_{\mathrm{R}}(u_2)\), by loc. cit. (iv).

(ii) is clear, since if P is a sheaf, \(\mathrm{Hom}(S,P) \to \mathrm{Hom}(R,P)\) is already an isomorphism for every refinement R of S.

(iii) Let \(u\) and \(v\) be two morphisms \(\mathrm{LP} \to F\) such that \(u\ell_{\mathrm{P}} = v\ell_{\mathrm{P}}\). To show \(u = v\), it suffices to see that \(uf = vf\) for every \(f : S \to \mathrm{LP}\) where S is representable. Now there exist a refinement R of S and a morphism \(g : R \to P\) with \(f = z_{\mathrm{R}}(g)\). Then \(uf\) and \(vf\) coincide on R with \(u\ell_{\mathrm{P}}g = v\ell_{\mathrm{P}}g\), hence coincide on R. If F is separated, we therefore have \(uf = vf\). Suppose now that F is a sheaf; we then have the commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ P \ar[r]^-{\ell_{\mathrm{P}}} \ar[d] & \mathrm{LP} \\ F \ar[r]_-{\sim} & \mathrm{LF} \ar[u] } \] which shows that \(\mathrm{Hom}(\ell_{\mathrm{P}},F)\) is surjective.

(iv) Let us show that if P is separated, \(\mathrm{P} \to \mathrm{LP}\) is a monomorphism. For this, it suffices to see that for every pair of morphisms \(u,v : S \to P\) (where S is representable) such that \(\ell_{\mathrm{P}}u = \ell_{\mathrm{P}}v\) we have \(u = v\). Now loc. cit. (iii) shows that \(u\) and \(v\) coincide on a refinement of S, hence coincide since P is separated. Let us show finally that LP is a sheaf. Since we already know by (i) that it is a separated presheaf, it suffices to see that for every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\), every refinement R of S and every morphism \(h : R \to \mathrm{LP}\), there exists a morphism \(u : S \to \mathrm{LP}\) with \(ui_{\mathrm{R}} = h\). Now \(R' = P \times_{\mathrm{LP}} R\) is a refinement of R, since P is a refinement of LP, so R' is a refinement of S. Put \(u = z_{\mathrm{R}'}(h')\). \[ \xymatrix{ P \ar[r]^-{\ell_{\mathrm{P}}} & \mathrm{LP} \\ R' \ar[u]^-{h'} \ar[r]_-{j} & R \ar[u]_-{h} \ar[r]_-{i_{\mathrm{R}}} & S \ar[uul]_-{u} } \] We have \(ui_{\mathrm{R}'} = \ell_{\mathrm{P}}h' = hj\), whence \(ui_{\mathrm{R}}j = hj\). Since R' is a refinement of R and since LP is separated, 4.3.2 shows that \(ui_{\mathrm{R}} = h\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.3.12

Let F be a sheaf and R a sub-\(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-object of F. Then R is a refinement of F if and only if we have a commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ R \ar@{^{(}->}[r] \ar[d]_-{\ell_{\mathrm{R}}} & F \\ \mathrm{LR} \ar[ur]_-{\simeq} & } \]

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 4.3.13

If J'(S) is a cofinal subset of J(S), we have \[ \mathrm{Hom}(S,\mathrm{LP}) = \varinjlim_{R\in J'(S)} \mathrm{Hom}(R,P). \] In particular, let \(S \mapsto R(S)\) be a pretopology generating the given topology. The functor L can be described using the covering families belonging to R(S). By spelling out the formula above, one recovers the construction of [MA].

Denote by \(i\) the inclusion functor \(\widetilde{\mathscr{C}} \to \widehat{\mathscr{C}}\). From proposition 4.3.11 results the following theorem:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.3.14

There exists a unique functor \(a : \widehat{\mathscr{C}} \to \widetilde{\mathscr{C}}\) such that the following diagram is commutative \[ \xymatrix{ \widehat{\mathscr{C}} \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{L}} \ar[d]_-{a} & \widehat{\mathscr{C}} \ar[d]^-{\mathrm{L}} \\ \widetilde{\mathscr{C}} \ar[r]_-{i} & \widehat{\mathscr{C}} } \] i.e. for every presheaf P, \(\mathrm{L}(\mathrm{L}(\mathrm{P}))\) is a sheaf. The functors \(i\) and \(a\) are adjoint to each other: for every presheaf P and every sheaf F we have an isomorphism, functorial in P and F, \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{\mathscr{C}}}(\mathrm{P},i(\mathrm{F})) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}_{\widetilde{\mathscr{C}}}(a(\mathrm{P}),\mathrm{F}), \] that is, \[ \mathrm{Hom}(\mathrm{P},\mathrm{F}) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}(a(\mathrm{P}),\mathrm{F}). \]

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.3.15

The sheaf \(a(P)\) is said to be associated with the presheaf P.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 4.3.16

Since the functor L is constructed by means of projective limits and filtered inductive limits, it commutes with finite projective limits.

Moreover, if we identify \(\mathrm{L}(\mathrm{P}\times\mathrm{P})\) with \(\mathrm{LP}\times\mathrm{LP}\), the morphism \(\ell_{\mathrm{P}\times\mathrm{P}}\) is identified with \(\ell_{\mathrm{P}}\times\ell_{\mathrm{P}}\). It follows, for example, that if P is a presheaf of groups, LP is also canonically equipped with a structure of presheaf of groups and the canonical morphism \(\mathrm{P} \to \mathrm{LP}\) is a group morphism. The same holds for the functor \(a\), which shows that if P is a presheaf of groups and F a sheaf of groups, we have an isomorphism \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\text{-gr.}}(\mathrm{P},i(\mathrm{F})) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}_{\widetilde{\mathscr{C}}\text{-gr.}}(a(\mathrm{P}),\mathrm{F}). \]

See [D] for more details.

If \(\mathscr{V}\) is an arbitrary category, a presheaf on \(\mathscr{C}\) with values in \(\mathscr{V}\) is a contravariant functor from \(\mathscr{C}\) to \(\mathscr{V}\). To define sheaves with values in \(\mathscr{V}\), we must first recall the definition of the projective limit of a functor. If \(\mathscr{R}\) and \(\mathscr{V}\) are two categories, and \[ F : \mathscr{R}^{\circ} \longrightarrow \mathscr{V} \] a contravariant functor from \(\mathscr{R}\) to \(\mathscr{V}\), we denote by \(\varprojlim F\) the object of \(\widehat{\mathscr{V}}\) defined as follows: \[ \varprojlim F(X) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{\mathscr{V}}}(X,\varprojlim F) = \varprojlim_{U \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{R}} \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{V}}(F(U),X) = \mathrm{Hom}(c_X,F), \] where X is a variable object of \(\mathscr{R}\), where \(c_X\) denotes the contravariant functor from \(\mathscr{R}\) to \(\mathscr{V}\) which sends every object of \(\mathscr{R}\) to X and every arrow of \(\mathscr{R}\) to \(\mathrm{id}_X\), and where the last Hom is taken in the category \(\mathbf{Hom}(\mathscr{R}^{\circ},\mathscr{V})\). If \(\mathscr{R}\) has a final object \(\underline{e}_{\mathscr{R}}\), we have \(\varprojlim F = F(\underline{e}_{\mathscr{R}})\). If \(\mathscr{V}\) is the category of sets, the functor \(\varprojlim\) is identified with the functor \(\Gamma\).

If S is an object of \(\mathscr{C}\) and R a sieve of S, denote by \(\mathscr{R}\) the full subcategory of \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\) whose set of objects is E(R) and by \(i_{\mathrm{R}} : \mathscr{R} \to \mathscr{C}_{/S}\) the canonical functor. If P is a presheaf on \(\mathscr{C}\) with values in \(\mathscr{V}\), it defines a functor \(\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{S}} : (\mathscr{C}_{/S})^{\circ} \to \mathscr{V}\). The functor \(i_{\mathrm{R}}\) induces a morphism of \(\widehat{\mathscr{V}}\) \[ \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{S}) = \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{S}}(\mathrm{S}) = \varprojlim \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{S}} \longrightarrow \varprojlim(\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{S}} \circ i_{\mathrm{R}}). \] We denote by \(\varprojlim_R P\) this last object of \(\widehat{\mathscr{V}}\). By virtue of 4.1.5, definition 4.3.1 generalizes to the following

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.3.18

The presheaf P on \(\mathscr{C}\) with values in \(\mathscr{V}\) is said to be separated (resp. a sheaf) if for every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\) and every \(R \in J(S)\), the canonical morphism of \(\widehat{\mathscr{V}}\) \[ \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{S}) \longrightarrow \varprojlim_R P \] is a monomorphism (resp. an isomorphism).

In the case where \(\mathscr{V}\) is the category (Gr.) of groups (or any other category of sets equipped with algebraic structures defined by finite projective limits), one can see (cf. [D]) that there is an equivalence between the following notions: a presheaf on \(\mathscr{C}\) with values in (Gr.) whose underlying presheaf of sets is a sheaf, and a group in the category of sheaves of sets. Taking these identifications into account, we shall always regard sheaves with values in a category of sets equipped with algebraic structures defined by finite projective limits as sheaves of sets equipped, in the category \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\), with the corresponding algebraic structure.
4.4. Exactness properties of the category of sheaves

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.4.1

(i) Arbitrary projective limits exist in \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\); "they are computed in \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)", i.e. the inclusion functor \(i : \widetilde{\mathscr{C}} \to \widehat{\mathscr{C}}\) commutes with projective limits: if \((\mathrm{X}_\alpha)\) is a projective system of sheaves, the presheaf \[ \varprojlim i(\mathrm{X}_\alpha) : \mathrm{S} \longmapsto \varprojlim \mathrm{X}_\alpha(\mathrm{S}) \] is a sheaf and we have \(i(\varprojlim \mathrm{X}_\alpha) = \varprojlim i(\mathrm{X}_\alpha)\).
