(ii) Arbitrary inductive limits exist in \(\widetilde{\mathscr{C}}\): if \((\mathrm{X}_\alpha)\) is an inductive system of sheaves, we have \[ \varinjlim \mathrm{X}_\alpha = a(\varinjlim i(\mathrm{X}_\alpha)) \] where \(\varinjlim i(\mathrm{X}_\alpha)\) is the presheaf inductive limit of the \(i(\mathrm{X}_\alpha)\): \[ \varinjlim i(\mathrm{X}_\alpha) : \mathrm{S} \longmapsto \varinjlim \mathrm{X}_\alpha(\mathrm{S}). \]

(iii) The functor \(a : \widehat{\mathscr{C}} \to \widetilde{\mathscr{C}}\) commutes with arbitrary inductive limits and with finite projective limits.

Assertions (i) and (ii) follow formally from the adjunction formula (4.3.14), and (iii) has already been pointed out in 4.3.16.

blocks.title: Scholium
blocks.html:
Scholium 4.4.2

This theorem allows us to use the following method to prove in \(\widetilde{\mathscr{C}}\) an assertion involving simultaneously arbitrary inductive limits and finite projective limits (for example: "every epimorphism is universal effective", cf. below). One begins by proving the corresponding assertion in the category of sets, then extends it "argument by argument" to the category of presheaves. Next, one uses the preceding theorem to pass from the category of presheaves to the category of sheaves. We shall see many examples of this method in what follows (4.4.3, 4.4.6, 4.4.9, etc...).

Let us note finally that the assertions relating to the category of presheaves are formally corollaries of the assertions relating to the category of sheaves. It suffices indeed to take as topology the coarsest ("chaotic") topology, that is, the topology defined by \(J(S) = \{S\}\) for every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\); every functor is indeed a sheaf for this topology.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.4.3

Let \(\mathscr{F} = \{F_i \to F\}\) be a family of morphisms of sheaves. The following conditions are equivalent:

(i) \(\mathscr{F}\) is an epimorphic family.

(ii) \(\mathscr{F}\) is a universal effective epimorphic family (1.13).

(iii) \(\mathscr{F}\) is covering (4.2.3).

(iv) The image sheaf of \(\mathscr{F}\) (that is, the sheaf associated with the image presheaf of \(\mathscr{F}\) (4.1.2)) is F.

The equivalence of (iii) and (iv) follows from 4.3.12. The other equivalences will follow from the following lemmas.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.4.4

Let \(f : X \to Y\) be a monomorphism of sheaves which is an epimorphism. Then \(f\) is an isomorphism.

The lemma is clear, first of all, in the category of sets. Let us prove it next in the category of presheaves. Consider the presheaf \[ V : S \longmapsto Y(S)\underset{X(S)}{\amalg}Y(S)\ ; \]

it is the amalgamated sum of Y and Y over X in the category of presheaves. If \(X \to Y\) is an epimorphism of presheaves, the first coordinate morphism \(Y \to V\) is an isomorphism, hence \(Y(S) \to V(S)\) is an isomorphism for each S, hence \(X(S) \to Y(S)\) is an epimorphism of sets, which settles the question, since it is on the other hand a monomorphism.

Let us finally place ourselves in the category of sheaves. The amalgamated sum Z of the sheaves Y and Y over X is the sheaf associated with V by 4.4.1. Now we have the commutative triangle \[ \xymatrix{ Y \ar[rr] \ar[dr] & & Z \\ & V \ar[ur] & } \]

If we know that the presheaf V is separated, we shall know that \(V \to Z\) is a monomorphism (4.3.11 (iv)). Since \(Y \to Z\) is an isomorphism by hypothesis, \(Y \to V\) will be one as well. The morphism \(X \to Y\) will therefore also be an epimorphism of presheaves, which proves the lemma, once we have checked that V is separated. This follows from:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.4.5

(i) The direct sum presheaf of a family of separated presheaves is separated.

(ii) Let \[ \xymatrix{ X \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^-{u} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_-{v} & Y } \] be an equivalence relation in the category of presheaves. Let \(w : Y \to Z\) be the quotient. If \(u \times v : X \to Y \times Y\) is a monomorphism, if X is a sheaf and if Y is separated, then Z is separated.

(iii) Consider an amalgamated sum in the category of presheaves, where \(u\) and \(u'\) are monomorphisms: \[ \xymatrix{ & Y \ar[dr]^-{u} & \\ X \ar[ur]^-{u} \ar[dr]_-{u'} & & V \\ & Y' \ar[ur]_-{u'} & } \]

If Y and Y' are separated presheaves, and X a sheaf, then V is separated.

(i) is proved at once; (iii) is proved without difficulty by remarking that for every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}(\mathscr{C})\), \[ \xymatrix{ & Y(S) \ar[dr] & \\ X(S) \ar[ur] \ar[dr] & & Z(S) \\ & Y'(S) \ar[ur] & } \] is an amalgamated sum in the category of sets. To prove (ii), we must prove that two morphisms from an S to Z coinciding on a refinement R of S are equal. Now every morphism from a representable S to Z lifts, by the construction of Z, to a morphism from S to Y. One then sees at once that it suffices to prove that for every presheaf U and every pair of morphisms \(f,g : U \to Y\) such that \(wf = wg\), there exists a unique morphism \(U \to X\) with \(uf = vg\). Now to do this, it suffices to prove the corresponding assertion for the composites of these morphisms with every \(T \to U\) where T is representable; we may therefore assume \(U = T\) representable. But the diagram \(X(T) \rightrightarrows Y(T) \to Z(T)\) is a cokernel in the category of sets, X(T) being an equivalence relation in Y(T), which completes the proof.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.4.6

A covering family is universal effective epimorphic.

Since the notion of covering family is stable under base extension, it suffices to show that every covering family is effective epimorphic. So let \(\{F_i \to F\}\) be a covering family. Consider the presheaf G, the image of this family. We have a diagram \[ \xymatrix{ F_i \ar[r] & G \ar[rr] \ar[dr] & & F \\ & & a(G) \ar[ur]_-{\simeq} & } \]

The family of morphisms of presheaves \(\{F_i \to G\}\) is effective epimorphic, for this is a verification which is made argument by argument, and in the category of sets, effective epimorphic family simply means surjective family. Since \(G \to F\) is a monomorphism, the fiber products \(F_i \times_G F_j\) and \(F_i \times_F F_j\) are the same. Moreover, for every sheaf H, we have \[ \mathrm{Hom}(G,H) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}(F,H). \] It follows that the given family is indeed an effective epimorphic family of sheaves.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.4.7

Every family of morphisms of sheaves \(\{F_i \to F\}\) factors into a covering family \(\{F_i \to G\}\) and a monomorphism \(G \to F\).

It suffices indeed to take for G the image sheaf of the given family.

Proof of the theorem: we saw in 4.4.6 that (iii) \(\Rightarrow\) (ii), and we evidently have (ii) \(\Rightarrow\) (i). Finally, let \(\{F_i \to F\}\) be an epimorphic family; by 4.4.7, it factors into a covering family followed by a monomorphism. But the latter, being dominated by an epimorphic family, is an epimorphism, hence an isomorphism by 4.4.4.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 4.4.7

Since the image presheaf of the family \(\mathscr{F}\) is separated, the construction of the associated sheaf shows that the conditions of the theorem are also equivalent to the following:

(v) For every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\), every \(f \in F(S)\) is locally in the image of \(\mathscr{F}\), that is:

(vi) For every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\) and every \(f \in F(S)\), the set of \(S' \to S\) such that the image of \(f\) in \(F(S')\) is in the image of one of the \(F_i(S')\) is a refinement of S.

(vii) (If the topology is defined by a pretopology). For every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\) and every \(f \in F(S)\), there exists a family \(\{S_j \to S\} \in R(S)\) such that for every \(j\) the image \(f_j\) of \(f\) in \(F(S_j)\) is in the image of one of the \(F_i(S_j)\).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 4.4.8

If the sheaf F is representable, the preceding conditions are also equivalent to

(viii) The set of \(T \to F\) (\(T \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\)) such that there exist an \(i\) and a commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ F_i \ar[rr] & & F \\ & T \ar[ul] \ar[ur] & } \] is a refinement of F.

Indeed, if (viii) is satisfied, the image presheaf of the \(F_i\) dominates a refinement of F, which implies that the family is covering. Conversely, one applies (vi) to \(\mathrm{id}_F \in F(F)\).

This condition can be expressed in picturesque language as follows: locally on F, there exists an \(i\) such that \(F_i \to F\) has a section. In particular a morphism \(G \to F\), where G is a sheaf and F a representable sheaf, will be covering if and only if it has a section locally (on F).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.4.9

Every equivalence relation in \(\widetilde{\mathscr{C}}\) is universal effective (3.3.3): let R be a \(\widetilde{\mathscr{C}}\)-equivalence relation in the sheaf X; then the sheaf associated with the separated presheaf \[ i(X)/i(R) : S \longmapsto X(S)/R(S) \] is a universal effective quotient of X by R.

Let X/R be the quotient sheaf of X by R, which exists by 4.4.1 (ii): \(\mathrm{X/R} = a(i(\mathrm{X})/i(\mathrm{R}))\). We must show that \(\mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{X/R}\) is a universal effective epimorphism, and that the morphism \(f : R \to X \times_{X/R} X\) is an isomorphism. The first assertion has already been proved (4.4.3). As for \(f\), it comes, by applying the functor \(a\), from the morphism \(i(R) \to i(X) \times_{i(X/R)} i(X)\) or, since \(i(X)/i(R)\) is separated (4.4.5 (ii)) and hence \(i(X)/i(R) \to i(X/R)\) is a monomorphism, from the canonical morphism \(i(R) \to i(X) \times_{i(X)/i(R)} i(R)\).

We are thus reduced to proving the same assertion in the category of presheaves. But \(i(X)/i(R)\) is the presheaf \(S \mapsto X(S)/R(S)\) and we are reduced to proving the analogous assertion in the category of sets, where it is immediate.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.4.10

Under the conditions of 4.4.9, let Y be a subsheaf of X. Denote by \(R_Y\) the equivalence relation induced in Y by R. Then the canonical morphism (3.1.6) \[ \mathrm{Y}/\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{Y}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{X}/\mathrm{R} \] is a monomorphism: it identifies \(\mathrm{Y}/\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{Y}}\) with a subsheaf of X/R, which is the image sheaf of the composite morphism \[ \mathrm{Y} \longrightarrow \mathrm{X} \longrightarrow \mathrm{X}/\mathrm{R}. \]

The morphism of presheaves \[ i(\mathrm{Y})/i(\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{Y}}) = i(\mathrm{X})/i(\mathrm{R})_{i(\mathrm{Y})} \longrightarrow i(\mathrm{X})/i(\mathrm{R}) \] is a monomorphism. Since the functor \(a\) is left exact (4.3.16), it takes monomorphisms to monomorphisms and \[ \mathrm{Y}/\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{Y}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{X}/\mathrm{R} \] is a monomorphism. The last assertion follows from the commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ Y \ar[r] \ar[d] & X \ar[d] \\ Y/R_Y \ar[r] & X/R } \] and from the fact that \(\mathrm{Y} \to \mathrm{Y}/\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{Y}}\) is covering.

By virtue of this proposition, we shall always identify \(\mathrm{Y}/\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{Y}}\) with a subsheaf of X/R.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.4.11

Let R be a \(\widetilde{\mathscr{C}}\)-equivalence relation in the sheaf X. For every subsheaf Y of X stable under R, denote by Y' the quotient \(\mathrm{Y}/\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{Y}}\) regarded as a subsheaf of \(\mathrm{X}' = \mathrm{X}/\mathrm{R}\). Then \(\mathrm{Y} = \mathrm{Y}' \times_{X'} X\), and the maps \(Y \mapsto Y/R_Y\) and \(Y' \mapsto T' \times_{X'} X\) realize a bijective correspondence between the set of subsheaves Y of X stable under R and the set of subsheaves Y' of X'.

If Y' is a subsheaf of X', then \(\mathrm{Y}' \times_{X'} X\) is a subsheaf of X stable under R. If Y' is obtained by passage to the quotient from a subsheaf Y of X, then \(\mathrm{Y}' \times_{X'} X\) dominates Y. It therefore suffices to show that if we have two subsheaves Y and \(Y_1\) of X, stable under R, with \(Y_1\) dominating Y, and if the quotients \(Y/R_Y\) and \(Y_1/R_{Y_1}\) are identical, then \(Y = Y_1\). We are evidently reduced to proving the same assertion in the case where \(Y_1 = X\). Denoting then by P (resp. Q) the presheaf \(i(X)/i(R)\) (resp. \(i(Y)/i(R_Y)\)), the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ X \ar[r] \ar[d] & P \ar[d] \\ Y \ar[r] & Q } \] is cartesian. Since we have a commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ P \ar@{^{(}->}[r] & a(P) \ar@{=}[d] \\ Q \ar@{^{(}->}[u] \ar@{^{(}->}[r] & a(Q) } \] the monomorphism \(Q \hookrightarrow P\) is covering, so Q is a refinement of P. By base change, Y is a refinement of X. Since X and Y are sheaves, this implies (4.3.12) \(\mathrm{Y} = \mathrm{X}\).

In particular, if Y is a subsheaf of X, and if \(Y' = Y/R_Y\), then the preceding correspondence defines a subsheaf \(\overline{Y}\) of X, stable under R, dominating Y and minimal for these properties, which is called the saturation of Y for the equivalence relation R.
4.5. The case of a topology coarser than the canonical topology
By 4.3.6 and 4.3.8, the following conditions are equivalent for a topology \(\mathscr{T}\) on \(\mathscr{C}\):
(i) \(\mathscr{T}\) is coarser than the canonical topology of \(\mathscr{C}\).
(ii) Every representable presheaf is a sheaf for \(\mathscr{T}\).
(iii) Every covering sieve for \(\mathscr{T}\) is universal effective epimorphic.
If \(\mathscr{T}\) is defined by a pretopology \(S \mapsto R(S)\), these conditions are also equivalent to
(iv) Every family \(\in R(S)\) is universal effective epimorphic.

In the case where these conditions are satisfied, the canonical functor \(\mathscr{C} \to \widehat{\mathscr{C}}\) factors through a functor \(j_{\mathscr{C}} = j : \mathscr{C} \to \widetilde{\mathscr{C}}\) (we shall also write \(j(S) = \widetilde{S}\ )\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.5.1

The functor \(j : \mathscr{C} \to \widetilde{\mathscr{C}}\) is fully faithful and commutes with arbitrary projective limits. It is in particular left exact and therefore preserves algebraic structures defined by finite projective limits.

This follows immediately from consideration of the commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \mathscr{C} \ar[rr] \ar[dr]_-{j} & & \widehat{\mathscr{C}} \\ & \widetilde{\mathscr{C}} \ar[ur]_-{i} & } \] and from 4.4.1 (i).

Before exhibiting other properties of the functor \(j\), we must define the induced topology on a category \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\). No longer necessarily assuming the given topology coarser than the canonical topology, this is done as follows: if C is a sieve of T in \(\mathscr{C}\) and if we have a morphism \(T \to S\), then C naturally defines a sieve of T in \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\) [for the definition of a sieve of T depends only on the category \(\mathscr{C}_{/T} = (\mathscr{C}_{/S})_{/T}\)]. If, for example, C is defined by the family \(\{T_i \to T\}\), then its image in \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\) is defined by the same family regarded as a family of morphisms of \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\). This said, the map \(T \mapsto J(T)\) defines a topology on \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\) called the topology induced by the given topology. With the definitions of [AS], 2.3, it is the coarsest of the topologies on \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\) for which the canonical functor \[ i_S : \mathscr{C}_{/S} \longrightarrow \mathscr{C} \] is a comorphism. Note that the identifications \[ (\mathscr{C}_{/S})_{/T} = \mathscr{C}_{/T} \] respect the topologies by definition.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.5.2

Let S be a representable sheaf on \(\mathscr{C}\) and \(F \to S\) a morphism of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\). For \(S' \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}_S(S',F)\) to be a separated presheaf (resp. a sheaf) on \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\), it is necessary and sufficient that F be a separated presheaf (resp. a sheaf) on \(\mathscr{C}\).

For every functor P, we have (I 4.1) \[ \mathrm{Hom}(P,F) = \coprod_{f\in\mathrm{Hom}(P,S)} \mathrm{Hom}_f(P,F). \] For every \(S' \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\) and every covering sieve C' of S', we have an isomorphism \[ \mathrm{Hom}(C',S) \xleftarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}(S',S), \] since S is a sheaf. The proposition follows immediately from these two formulas.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.5.3

The topology induced on \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\) by a topology on \(\mathscr{C}\) coarser than the canonical topology of \(\mathscr{C}\) is coarser than the canonical topology of \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.5.4

Suppose the given topology on \(\mathscr{C}\) is coarser than the canonical topology. For every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\), we have an equivalence of categories \[ \widetilde{\mathscr{C}}_{/\widetilde{S}} \to \widetilde{\mathscr{C}_{/S}}. \]

The following diagrams are commutative up to isomorphism (all unlabeled arrows are equivalences): \[ \xymatrix{ \mathscr{C}_{/S} \ar[r]^-{(j_{\mathscr{C}})_{/S}} \ar@{=}[d] & \widetilde{\mathscr{C}}_{/\widetilde{S}} \ar[r]^-{(i_{\mathscr{C}})_{/S}} \ar[d]^-{\wr} & \widehat{\mathscr{C}}_{/\widetilde{S}} \ar[r]^-{(a_{\mathscr{C}})_{/S}} \ar[d]^-{\wr} & \widetilde{\mathscr{C}}_{/\widetilde{S}} \ar[d]^-{\wr} \\ \mathscr{C}_{/S} \ar[r]_-{j_{\mathscr{C}_S}} & \widetilde{\mathscr{C}_S} \ar[r]_-{i_{\mathscr{C}_S}} & \widehat{\mathscr{C}_S} \ar[r]_-{a_{\mathscr{C}_S}} & \widetilde{\mathscr{C}_S} } \] \[ \xymatrix{ (\widehat{\mathscr{C}}_{/\widetilde{S}})_{/\widetilde{T}} \ar[r] \ar[d]_-{\wr} & (\widetilde{\mathscr{C}_{/S}})_{/T} \ar[dr]^-{\simeq} & \\ \widehat{\mathscr{C}_{/T}} \ar[r] & \widetilde{\mathscr{C}_{/T}} \ar[r] & (\widetilde{\mathscr{C}_{/S}})_{/T} } \]

The commutativity of the first two squares follows from the definition of the equivalence \(\widetilde{\mathscr{C}}_{/\widetilde{S}} \to \widetilde{\mathscr{C}_{/S}}\). To prove the commutativity of the last one, it must be seen that the following square is commutative: \[ \xymatrix{ \widehat{\mathscr{C}}_{/\widetilde{S}} \ar[r]^-{L'} \ar[d] & \widehat{\mathscr{C}}_{/\widetilde{S}} \ar[d] \\ \widehat{\mathscr{C}_S} \ar[r]_-{L''} & \widehat{\mathscr{C}_S} } \] where L' is the restriction of the functor \(\mathrm{L}_{\mathscr{C}}\) to \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}_{/\widetilde{S}}\) and L'' the functor \(\mathrm{L}_{\mathscr{C}_S}\); this is seen easily by going back to the definition of the functors L (after 4.3.9). As for the second diagram, it is none other than the restriction to the categories of sheaves of the corresponding diagram on the categories of presheaves (Exposé I, no. 1), which is commutative.

blocks.title: Scholium
blocks.html:
Scholium 4.5.5

The various assertions of this section show that in the case where the given topology is coarser than the canonical topology, one may identify \(\mathscr{C}\) with a full subcategory of \(\widetilde{\mathscr{C}}\), itself a full subcategory of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\), and that in this identification one may indulge in the abuses of language customary as regards \(\mathscr{C} \to \widehat{\mathscr{C}}\), justified by the preceding commutativities. Let us note explicitly that the first diagram of 4.5.4 shows that one may use the functor \(a\) without special precautions.

We shall see in the next section that the identification of \(\mathscr{C}\) with a full subcategory of \(\widetilde{\mathscr{C}}\) (contrary to what happened for \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)) commutes with the formation of certain inductive limits, and we shall then say how to use this fact.
From now on, and unless expressly stated otherwise, we shall assume the given topology coarser than the canonical topology, and we shall systematically make the identifications set out above.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.5.6

Let F and G be two sheaves over S and \(f : F \to G\) an S-morphism. The following conditions on \(f\) are equivalent:

(i) \(f\) is a monomorphism (resp. an epimorphism, resp. an isomorphism) in \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\).

(ii) \(f\) is a monomorphism (resp. an epimorphism, resp. an isomorphism) in \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}_{/S} = \widetilde{\mathscr{C}}_{/S}\).

For monomorphism and isomorphism, this is obvious (it is a question of presheaves). For epimorphism, it follows from the description of epimorphisms as covering morphisms and from the fact that, by the definition of the induced topology, these are the same in \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\) and \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}_{/S}\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.5.7

Let \(f : F \to G\) be a morphism of sheaves. The following conditions are equivalent:

(i) \(f\) is a monomorphism (resp. an epimorphism, resp. an isomorphism).

(ii) For each \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\), \(f_S : F_S \to G_S\) is locally a monomorphism (resp. an epimorphism, resp. an isomorphism), that is:

(iii) For each \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\), the set of \(T \to S\) such that \(F_T \to G_T\) is a monomorphism (resp. an epimorphism, resp. an isomorphism) is a refinement of S.

If the given topology is defined by a pretopology, these conditions are also equivalent to the following:

(iv) For each \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\), there exists a covering family \(\{S_i \to S\} \in R(S)\) such that for every \(i\), \(F_{S_i} \to G_{S_i}\) is a monomorphism (resp. an epimorphism, resp. an isomorphism).

If the category \(\mathscr{C}\) has a final object \(e\), one may content oneself with taking \(S = e\) in conditions (ii), (iii) and (iv).

We evidently have (ii) \(\Leftrightarrow\) (iii) \(\Leftrightarrow\) (iv). To prove the equivalence of (i) and (ii), as well as the supplement concerning the final object, we must show that (ii) \(\Rightarrow\) (i) and that the notions under consideration are stable under base extension. Let us first prove this last point. For monomorphism and isomorphism, it is obvious (it is a question of presheaves). For epimorphism, it follows from the fact that every epimorphism of sheaves is universal (4.4.3). Let us show finally that (ii) implies (i). Suppose that \(f_S : F_S \to G_S\) is locally a monomorphism (resp. an isomorphism). There then exists a covering sieve C of S such that for every \(T \to C\), \(f_T\) is a monomorphism (resp. an isomorphism). Since a projective limit of monomorphisms (resp. isomorphisms) is one, \(\mathrm{Hom}(C,f)\) will be a monomorphism (resp. an isomorphism) (cf. 4.1.3). The commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{Hom}(C,F) \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{Hom}(C,f)} & \mathrm{Hom}(C,G) \\ F(S) \ar[u]^-{\wr} \ar[r]_-{f(S)} & G(S) \ar[u]_-{\wr} } \] then shows that \(f(S)\) is injective (resp. bijective). Suppose finally that \(F \to G\) is locally an epimorphism and let \(H \subset G\) be the image of \(f\). For each \(S \to G\), \(H \times_G S\) is the image of \(f \times_G S : F \times_G S \to S\). To show that \(f\) is an epimorphism, we must show that H is a refinement of G, that is, that \(H \times_G S\) is a refinement of S for each S. But since this is so after every base change \(T \to S\) of a refinement of S (if \(f \times_G S\) is locally covering), \(H \times_G S\) is indeed a refinement of S (Axiom (T 2)).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.5.8

Let F and G be two sheaves over S and \(f : F \to G\) an S-morphism. For \(f\) to be a monomorphism, resp. an epimorphism, resp. an isomorphism, it is necessary and sufficient that it be one locally on S.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 4.5.9

The proof of the proposition shows that it remains valid, for the part concerning monomorphisms (resp. isomorphisms), when we assume only that F is a separated presheaf (resp. sheaf) and G an arbitrary presheaf (resp. separated presheaf).

Let us return provisionally to the case of an arbitrary topology and state a definition.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.5.10

Let \(G \to F\) be a morphism of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\). We say that G is a relative sheaf over F if for each F-functor H and each refinement R of H, the canonical map \[ (+) \qquad \mathrm{Hom}_F(R,G) \xleftarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}_F(H,G) \] is bijective.

Proposition 4.5.2 generalizes at once:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.5.11

If F is a sheaf, G is a relative sheaf over F if and only if it is a sheaf.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.5.12

In the situation \(X \to T \to S\) (where X,T,S are three objects of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)), if X is a relative sheaf over T, then \(\mathrm{U} = \prod_{T/S} X\) is a relative sheaf over S.

Indeed, for every S-functor Y we have \[ \mathrm{Hom}_S(Y,U) = \mathrm{Hom}_T(T\underset{S}{\times}Y,X). \] If C is a sieve of Y, then \(T \times_S C\) is a sieve of \(T \times_S Y\); we conclude at once.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.5.13

The presheaves \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{T/S}(X,Y)\), \(\underline{\mathrm{Isom}}_S(X,Y),\ldots\), are sheaves when the arguments occurring in them are sheaves as well.

Indeed, all these presheaves are constructed by means of fiber products and presheaves \(\prod\) (I 1.7 and II 1). It suffices to check the result for a presheaf \(\prod_{T/S} X\); in this case, the assertion follows from 4.5.11 and 4.5.12.
4.6. Description of the quotient of a sheaf by an equivalence relation
Recall that we assume the given topology \(\mathscr{T}\) coarser than the canonical topology.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.6.1

Let \(\xymatrix{ R \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^-{p_1} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_-{p_2} & X }\) be a \(\widetilde{\mathscr{C}}\)-equivalence relation in the sheaf X. Let \(F \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\) be defined as follows: for each S of \(\mathscr{C}\), F(S) is the set of sub-S-sheaves Z of \(X_S\), stable under \(R_S\), whose quotient by \(R_Z\) is S (that is, such that the diagram \(R_Z \rightrightarrows Z \to S\) is exact). Then for every sheaf Y, Hom(Y,F) is identified with the set of sub-Y-sheaves of \(X \times Y\) stable under \(R \times Y\) whose quotient is Y. In particular the subsheaf R of \(X \times X\) corresponds to an element \(p\) of Hom(X,F) and the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ R \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^-{p_1} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_-{p_2} & X \ar[r]^-{p} & F } \] is exact, hence identifies F with the quotient sheaf X/R.

Indeed, put \(Q = X/R\). For every sheaf Y and every morphism \(f \in \mathrm{Hom}(Y,Q)\) corresponding to a section \(s : Y \hookrightarrow Y \times Q\), consider the diagram \[ (*) \qquad \xymatrix{ R\times Y \ar@<0.6ex>[r] \ar@<-0.6ex>[r] & X\times Y \ar[r] & Q\times Y \\ & Z \ar[u] \ar[r] & Y \ar[u]_-{s} } \] where the square is cartesian. It is immediate by 4.4.11 that Z is a sub-Y-sheaf of \(X \times Y\), stable under \(R \times Y\), whose quotient is Y, and that, conversely, every Z of this type comes from a unique section of \(Q \times Y\) over Y. Taking first Y representable, we deduce an isomorphism \(Q \simeq F\). Taking next Y arbitrary, we deduce the announced form of Hom(Y,F). Considering finally the canonical morphism \(X \to Q\), we see at once that it corresponds to the sub-X-sheaf R of \(X \times X\), which completes the proof.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.6.2

Let G be an arbitrary subfunctor of F such that \(\mathrm{Hom}(X,G) \subset \mathrm{Hom}(X,F)\) contains \(p\). Then the canonical morphism \(X \to F\) factors through G. Since \(X \to F\) is covering (4.4.9 and 4.4.3), it follows that G is a refinement of F. In particular, every subsheaf G of F satisfying the preceding condition is equal to F (4.3.12).

We shall now take an interest in the case where X and R are representable. Let us first introduce some terminology. Besides the conditions (a) to (d) introduced in 3.4.1, we shall use other conditions on a family (M) of morphisms of \(\mathscr{C}\), which we state below, recalling the conditions (a) to (c) already given, for the sake of completeness.
(a) (M) is stable under base extension.
(b) The composite of two elements of (M) is in (M).
(c) Every isomorphism belongs to (M).
(\(\mathrm{d}_{\mathscr{T}}\)) Every element of (M) is covering.

(\(\mathrm{e}_{\mathscr{T}}\)) Let \(f : X \to Y\) be a morphism of \(\mathscr{C}\). If there exists a refinement R of Y such that for every \(Y' \to R\), \(X \times_Y Y' \to Y'\) belongs to (M), then \(f\) belongs to (M).
Recall that (a) and (b) imply
(a') The cartesian product of two elements of (M) belongs to (M).
On the other hand (a) and \((\mathrm{d}_{\mathscr{T}})\) imply, by 4.3.9:
(d') Every element of (M) is a universal effective epimorphism.

The preceding conditions are satisfied by the family of covering morphisms, denoted \((\mathrm{M}_{\mathscr{T}})\), when \(\mathscr{C}\) has fiber products. Indeed (a) follows from (C 1) (4.2.3), (b) from (C 2), (c) from (C 4), \((\mathrm{d}_{\mathscr{T}})\) from the definition, and \((\mathrm{e}_{\mathscr{T}})\) from (C 5). The results we shall establish for a family satisfying these conditions will apply in particular to the family \((\mathrm{M}_{\mathscr{T}})\). In particular, one may take for \(\mathscr{T}\) the canonical topology and for (M) the family of universal effective epimorphisms.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.6.5

Let (M) be a family of morphisms satisfying the preceding properties (a) to \((\mathrm{e}_{\mathscr{T}})\). Let R be a \(\mathscr{C}\)-equivalence relation in \(X \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\), of type (M). Let \(\widetilde{X}\) be the sheaf defined by X, \(\widetilde{R}\) the \(\widetilde{\mathscr{C}}\)-equivalence relation in \(\widetilde{X}\) defined by R, and \(\widetilde{X}/\widetilde{R}\) the quotient sheaf. For R to be (M)-effective, it is necessary and sufficient that \(\widetilde{X}/\widetilde{R}\) be representable. If this is so, \(\widetilde{X}/\widetilde{R}\) is representable by the quotient X/R.

Suppose first that R is (M)-effective and write \(Y = X/R\). The canonical morphism \(p : X \to Y\) belongs to (M), hence is covering by \((\mathrm{d}_{\mathscr{T}})\). The corresponding morphism \[ \widetilde{p} : \widetilde{X} \longrightarrow \widetilde{X}/\widetilde{R} \] is therefore a universal effective epimorphism of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\) (4.4.3), hence identifies \(\widetilde{X}/\widetilde{R}\) with the quotient of \(\widetilde{X}\) by the equivalence relation R' defined in \(\widetilde{X}\) by \(\widetilde{p}\). Since the canonical functor \(\mathscr{C} \to \widehat{\mathscr{C}}\) commutes with fiber products, R' is none other than \(\widetilde{R}\), since R is the equivalence relation defined by \(p\).

Conversely, suppose \(\widetilde{X}/\widetilde{R}\) representable by an object Y of \(\mathscr{C}\). Let \(p : X \to Y\) be the morphism deduced from the canonical morphism \(\widetilde{X} \to \widetilde{X}/\widetilde{R}\); it is a covering morphism by 4.4.3. It is clear, as before, that R is the equivalence relation defined by \(p\). It only remains to show that \(p \in (\mathrm{M})\). Now the cartesian square \[ \xymatrix{ R \ar[r]^-{\sim} \ar[dr]_-{p_2} & X\underset{Y}{\times}X \ar[r] \ar[d]^-{\mathrm{pr}_2} & X \ar[d]^-{p} \\ & X \ar[r]_-{p} & Y } \] shows that \(p\) becomes \(p_2\), which is an element of (M), after base change by the covering morphism \(p\). We conclude by \((\mathrm{e}_{\mathscr{T}})\).

We are now in a position to state the main result of this section.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.6.6

Let (M) be a family of morphisms satisfying axioms (a) to \((\mathrm{e}_{\mathscr{T}})\) of 4.6.3. Let R be a \(\mathscr{C}\)-equivalence relation of type (M) in the object X of \(\mathscr{C}\). Consider the functor \(F \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\) defined as follows: F(S) is the set of sub-S-sheaves Z of \(X_S\) stable under \(R_S\) and such that the induced equivalence relation has quotient S. Let \(F_0\) be the subfunctor of F defined as follows: \(F_0(S)\) consists of the \(Z \in F(S)\) which are representable, that is, of the sub-\(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\)-objects of \(X_S\) stable under \(R_S\) and such that the induced equivalence relation is (M)-effective and has quotient S (that is, \(R_Z \simeq Z \times_S Z\) and \(Z \to S\) belongs to (M)). Then:

(i) The morphism \(p \in \mathrm{Hom}(X,F) = F(X)\) defined by the subobject R of \(X \times X\) identifies F with the quotient sheaf of X by R.

(ii) The following conditions are equivalent:

a) F is representable.

b) \(F_0\) is representable.

c) R is (M)-effective.

Under these conditions, \(F = F_0 = X/R\).

(iii) Let (N) be a family of morphisms stable under base change, such that for every covering family \(\{S_i \to S\}\) and every family \(\{T_i \to S_i\}\) of morphisms of (N), every descent datum on the \(T_i\) relative to \(\{S_i \to S\}\) is effective. Suppose X squarable and the morphism \(R \to X \times X\) an element of (N). Then \(F_0 = F\).

Proof. (i) has already been proved (4.6.1). (ii) We have seen the equivalence of a) and c) as well as \(F = X/R\). It remains to prove that b) or c) implies \(F_0 = F\). Note first, as is moreover asserted in the statement, that \(F_0\) is indeed a subfunctor of F; indeed for every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\) and every \(Z \in F_0(S)\), the morphism \(Z \to S\) is squarable, so \(Z \times_S S'\) is an element of \(F_0(S')\) for every \(S' \to S\). Since \(R \in F(X)\) belongs to \(F_0(X)\), 4.6.2 shows that b) \(\Rightarrow F_0 = F\). Suppose now that c) is satisfied. The morphism \(X \to Q\) belongs to (M) and, for every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\) and every \(Z \in F(S)\), the diagram (*) of 4.6.1 shows that \(Z = S \times_{(X/R \times S)} X \times S\) is representable, hence an element of \(F_0(S)\).

(iii) Let \(f \in \mathrm{Hom}(S,F)\) correspond to \(Z \in F(S)\). We must show that \(f\) factors through \(F_0\), that is, that Z is representable. This is clear, first of all, if \(f\) factors through X, by virtue of:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.6.7

Let \(x_0 \in X(S)\). The image of \(x_0\) in F(S) corresponds to the subsheaf Z of \(X_S\) defined by the two cartesian squares \[ \xymatrix{ X_S \ar[r]^-{(\mathrm{id}_{X_S},x_0)} & X_S\underset{S}{\times}X_S \ar[r] & X\times X \\ Z \ar[u] \ar[r] & R_S \ar[u] \ar[r] & R } \]

This lemma follows at once from the description of the morphism \(X \to F\).

Let us return to the proof of the theorem. If \(f\) factors through X, then Z is representable and the morphism \(Z \to X_S\) an element of (N). In general, \(f\) does not necessarily factor through X; but, since \(X \to F\) is covering (4.4.3), there exist by 4.4.7 a covering family \(\{S_i \to S\}\) and for each \(i\) a morphism \(S_i \to X\) making the following diagram commute \[ \xymatrix{ X \ar[r] & F \\ S_i \ar[u] \ar[r] & S \ar[u]_-{f} } \]

By what precedes, the morphism \(f_i : S_i \to F\) defined by the preceding diagram belongs to \(\mathrm{Hom}(S_i,F_0)\) and corresponds to the subsheaf \(Z \times_S S_i\) of \(X_{S_i}\). The morphism \(Z \times_S S_i \to X_{S_i}\) is an element of (N) and the family \(X_{S_i} \to X_S\) is covering. It therefore only remains to establish:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.6.8

Let \(\{S_i \to S\}\) be a covering family and F a sheaf over S. Suppose that for each \(i\), the \(S_i\)-functor \(F \times_S S_i\) is representable by an object \(T_i\). Then the family of the \(T_i\) is equipped with a canonical descent datum relative to \(S_i \to S\). For F to be representable, it is necessary and sufficient that this datum be effective; if this is so, the "descended" object represents F.

Note first that by 4.4.3, \(S_i \to S\) is a universal effective epimorphic family in \(\widetilde{\mathscr{C}}\), hence a descent family in \(\widetilde{\mathscr{C}}\). If F is representable by the object T, then \(T \times_S S_i\) (regarded as a sheaf) is isomorphic to \(F \times_S S_i\), so the descent datum on the \(T_i\) is effective and the (unique) descended object is isomorphic to F. Conversely, suppose that the canonical descent datum on the \(T_i\) is effective and let T be the descended object. Since the family \(\{S_i \to S\}\) is a descent family in \(\widetilde{\mathscr{C}}\), there exists an S-morphism \(T \to F\) which by extension of the base to each \(S_i\) gives back the canonical morphism \(T_i \to F \times_S S_i\). This morphism is locally an isomorphism; since T and F are sheaves, it follows from 4.5.8 that it is an isomorphism.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.6.9

Let R be an (M)-effective equivalence relation in X. For every sheaf F, the map \[ \mathrm{Hom}(X/R,F) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}(X,F) \] identifies the first set with the part of the second consisting of the morphisms compatible with R.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.6.10

Let \(\mathscr{T}'\) be a topology coarser than \(\mathscr{T}\), for which the morphisms of (M) are covering. Under the conditions of 4.6.6 (iii), X/R is also the quotient sheaf of X by R in every topology intermediate between \(\mathscr{T}'\) and the canonical topology.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 4.6.11

If in the statement of 4.6.6 (iii) we assume in addition that, under the hypotheses of the statement, if T denotes the descended object, the morphism \(T \to S\) belongs to (N), then the inclusion morphisms \(Z \hookrightarrow X_S\) also belong to (N), as follows at once from the construction of Z by descent.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 4.6.12

The implications \[ c) \Rightarrow b) \Rightarrow a), \qquad c) \Rightarrow F_0 = F = X/R, \] were established without recourse to the "sufficiency" part of lemma 4.6.5, which is the only place where condition \((\mathrm{e}_{\mathscr{T}})\) is used. They therefore remain valid if (M) satisfies only conditions (a) to \((\mathrm{d}_{\mathscr{T}})\). An example of such a family (M) is that of covering squarable morphisms (compare with 4.6.4). In the case of the canonical topology, these are none other than the universal effective epimorphisms. We thus have:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.6.13

Let R be a universal effective equivalence relation in X. Then X/R is the quotient sheaf of X by R for the canonical topology. It represents the following functor: X/R(S) is the set of sub-\(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\)-objects Z of \(X_S\) stable under \(R_S\) and such that the induced equivalence relation is universal effective and has quotient S.
Likewise, for an arbitrary topology:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.6.14

Let (M) be the family of covering squarable morphisms. If R is an (M)-effective equivalence relation in X, then X/R is the quotient sheaf of X by R and represents the functor \(F_0\) of 4.6.6.

blocks.title: Scholium
blocks.html:
Scholium 4.6.15

We can now add the following clarifications to 4.5.5. Whereas in questions involving exclusively projective limits (fiber products, algebraic structures...), one may, by the results of Exposé I and 4.5.5, identify \(\mathscr{C}\) indifferently with a full subcategory of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\) or of \(\widetilde{\mathscr{C}}\), the same is not true of those which mix projective and inductive limits. In all questions involving both projective limits and inductive limits, in particular passages to the quotient (example: group structure on the quotient of a group by a normal subgroup), we shall regard the given category as embedded in the category of sheaves; thus if R is a \(\mathscr{C}\)-equivalence relation in the object X of \(\mathscr{C}\), X/R will denote the quotient sheaf of X by R (previously denoted \(j(X)/j(R)\)), and hence, in the case where this sheaf is representable, the object it represents. The preceding results show that in the most important cases, a quotient in \(\mathscr{C}\) will also be a quotient in the category of sheaves; in any case, we forbid ourselves the use of the notation X/R for a quotient in \(\mathscr{C}\) which would not coincide with the quotient in \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\) (for example one which would not be universal), thereby modifying the definitions of no. 3.

To study a problem of the above type, one thus places oneself first in the category of sheaves, where all the usual results are valid (cf. no. 4.4), then one specializes the results obtained to the original category, using the results of the present section and, when available, criteria for effectivity of descent. We shall see examples of this method in the following sections.
4.7. Use of effectivity criteria: isomorphism theorem

In this section, we give an example of the use of effectivity criteria. The initial data are a topology \(\mathscr{T}\) on \(\mathscr{C}\) (still coarser than the canonical topology), a family (M) of morphisms of \(\mathscr{C}\) satisfying axioms (a) to \((\mathrm{e}_{\mathscr{T}})\) of 4.6.3, and a family (N) of morphisms of \(\mathscr{C}\) liable to satisfy the following axioms:
(a) (N) is stable under base extension.

(\(\mathrm{f}_{\mathscr{T}}\)) "the morphisms of (N) descend by the given topology"; that is: for every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\), every covering family \(\{S_i \to S\}\) and every family \(\{T_i \to S_i\}\) of morphisms of (N), every descent datum on the \(T_i\) relative to \(\{S_i \to S\}\) is effective, and if T denotes the descended object, the morphism \(T \to S\) belongs to (N).

Let us point out at once an example of this situation, which will be treated later: \(\mathscr{C}\) is the category of schemes, \(\mathscr{T}\) the faithfully flat quasi-compact topology; (M) the family of faithfully flat quasi-compact morphisms, (N) the family of closed immersions, or that of quasi-compact immersions.
Let us recall the main result of 4.6.6 (taking 4.6.11 into account):

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.7.1

If X is a squarable object of \(\mathscr{C}\), R an equivalence relation of type (M) in X such that \(X \to R \times R\) is an element of (N), with (N) satisfying (a) and \((\mathrm{f}_{\mathscr{T}})\), then the quotient sheaf X/R is given by \[ \mathrm{X/R}(S) = \left\{\begin{array}{l} \text{set of sub-S-objects Z of } X_S, \text{ stable under } R_S, \text{ such that } Z \to X_S \\ \text{is an element of (N), that } Z \to S \text{ is covering (or an element of (M)) and} \\ \text{that } R_Z \simeq Z \underset{S}{\times} Z. \end{array}\right. \]

Let X be an object of \(\mathscr{C}\), R an (M)-effective equivalence relation in X, and write \(X' = X/R\). For every subobject Y' of X' such that the canonical morphism \(Y' \to X'\) is an element of (N), the subobject \(Y = Y' \times_{X'} X\) of X is stable under R, the canonical morphism \(Y \to X\) is an element of (N) (if (N) satisfies (a)), the equivalence relation induced in Y is (M)-effective (4.4.11 and 4.6.6) and its quotient is Y'. Conversely, let us show that every subobject Y of X, stable under R, such that the structural morphism \(X \to Y\) is an element of (N), is obtained in this way; indeed, if Y is stable under R, its two images in \(R = X \times_{X'} X\) are identical and Y is equipped with a descent datum relative to \(X \to X'\). To obtain the desired result, it therefore suffices that the family (N) satisfy the following axiom (implied by \((\mathrm{f}_{\mathscr{T}})\) and the conditions on (M)):

\((\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{M}})\) If \(Y \to X\) is an element of (N) and \(X \to X_1\) an element of (M), every descent datum on Y relative to \(X \to X_1\) is effective; if \(Y_1\) denotes the descended object, \(Y_1 \to X_1\) is an element of (N).

We thus have:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.7.2

Let R be an (M)-effective equivalence relation in X. Let (N) be a family of morphisms satisfying (a) and \((\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{M}})\). For every subobject Y of X, stable under R and such that the morphism \(Y \to X\) is an element of (N), the equivalence relation induced in Y by R is (M)-effective and the quotient \(\mathrm{Y}/\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{Y}} = \mathrm{Y}'\) is a subobject of \(\mathrm{X}' = \mathrm{X}/\mathrm{R}\) such that \(\mathrm{Y}' \to \mathrm{X}'\) is an element of (N). The map \(\mathrm{Y} \mapsto \mathrm{Y}'\) realizes a bijective correspondence between the set of subobjects Y of X, stable under R, such that the morphism \(\mathrm{Y} \to \mathrm{X}\) is an element of (N), and the set of subobjects Y' of X' such that the morphism \(\mathrm{Y}' \to \mathrm{X}'\) is an element of (N). The inverse map is \(\mathrm{Y}' \mapsto \mathrm{Y}' \times_{X'} \mathrm{X}\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.7.3

Suppose in addition that \(\mathrm{R} \to \mathrm{X} \times \mathrm{X}\) is an element of (N). Then, for every Y as in the statement, \(\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{Y}} \to \mathrm{Y} \times \mathrm{Y}\) is also an element of (N). If (N) satisfies (a) and \((\mathrm{f}_{\mathscr{T}})\), we therefore have \[ \mathrm{Y}'(\mathrm{S}) = \left\{\begin{array}{l} \text{set of sub-S-objects Z of } Y_S, \text{ stable under } R_S, \text{ such that } Z \to Y_S \text{ is an} \\ \text{element of (N) (then } Z \to X_S \text{ is also an element of (N)), that } Z \to S \text{ is} \\ \text{covering and that } R_Z \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } Z \underset{S}{\times} Z. \end{array}\right. \]

pages.title: 5. Passage to the quotient and algebraic structures

blocks.html: 5. Passage to the quotient and algebraic structures
5.1. Principal homogeneous bundles

Recall (III 1) that an object X with group of operators (on the right) H is said to be formally principal homogeneous under H if the canonical morphism (of functors) \[ X\times H \longrightarrow X\times X \] defined by \((x,h) \mapsto (x,xh)\) is an isomorphism. It comes to the same thing to say (cf. loc. cit.) that for every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\), X(S) is formally principal homogeneous under H(S), that is, empty or principal homogeneous under H(S). In particular, if we let H act on itself by translations (on the right), H becomes formally principal homogeneous under itself.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 5.1.1

The object X with group of operators H is said to be trivial if it is isomorphic (as an object with group of operators H) to H acted on by H by translations.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.1.2

Let X be formally principal homogeneous under H. We have an isomorphism \[ \Gamma(X) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Isom}_{\mathrm{H\text{-}obj.}}(\mathrm{H},\mathrm{X}). \] of principal homogeneous sets under \(\Gamma(H)\).

To every section \(x\) of X we associate the morphism from H to X defined set-theoretically by \(h \mapsto xh\). The stated assertion is immediate, by reduction to the set-theoretic case.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.1.3

We have an isomorphism of objects with operators H \[ X \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Isom}}_{\mathrm{H\text{-}obj.}}(H,X). \]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.1.4

For an object with group of operators to be trivial, it is necessary and sufficient that it be formally principal homogeneous and that it have a section.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 5.1.5

Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be a category equipped with a topology. We say that the S-object X with S-group of operators H is a principal homogeneous bundle under H if it is locally trivial, that is, if the following equivalent conditions are satisfied:

(i) The set of \(T \to S\) such that (the functor) \(X \times_S T\) is trivial under \(H \times_S T\) is a refinement of S.

(ii) There exists a covering family (for a pretopology defining the given topology) \(\{S_i \to S\}\) such that for each \(i\), the \(S_i\)-functor \(X \times_S S_i\) with \(S_i\)-group-functor of operators \(H \times_S S_i\) is trivial (\(=\) has a section over \(S_i\)).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.1.6

Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be a category equipped with a topology \(\mathscr{T}\). Let (M) be a family of morphisms of \(\mathscr{C}\) satisfying axioms (a) to \((\mathrm{e}_{\mathscr{T}})\) of 4.6.3. Let H be an S-group such that the structural morphism \(H \to S\) is an element of (M), and P an S-object with S-group of operators H. The following conditions are equivalent:

(i) P is a principal homogeneous bundle under H (definition 5.1.5).

(ii) P is formally principal homogeneous under H and the structural morphism \(\mathrm{P} \to \mathrm{S}\) is an element of (M).

(iii) There exists a morphism \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) belonging to (M) such that by extension of the base from S to S', P becomes trivial, that is, \(\mathrm{P} \times_S \mathrm{S}'\) is trivial under \(\mathrm{H} \times_S \mathrm{S}'\).

(iv) H acts freely on P, in an (M)-effective manner, and the quotient H/P is isomorphic to S.

Note first that (ii) and (iv) are equivalent, taking into account the fact that, in either case, \(\mathrm{P} \to \mathrm{S}\) is an element of (M), hence squarable, which ensures the representability of the fiber products \(\mathrm{H} \times_S \mathrm{P}\) and \(\mathrm{P} \times_S \mathrm{P}\). It is clear that (ii) implies (iii), for one may take as S' P itself, the hypothesis that P is formally principal homogeneous implying that \(\mathrm{P} \times_S \mathrm{P}\) is trivial under \(\mathrm{H} \times_S \mathrm{P}\) (5.1.4), since it has a section (the diagonal section). It is clear that (iii) implies (i), since \(\{\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\}\) is a covering family, by axiom \((d_{\mathscr{T}})\). It thus remains to show that (i) implies (ii). The morphism of sheaves \(\mathrm{P} \times_S \mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{P} \times_S \mathrm{P}\) is locally an isomorphism, hence an isomorphism (4.5.8); P is therefore formally principal homogeneous. The structural morphism \(\mathrm{P} \to \mathrm{S}\) is locally isomorphic to the structural morphism \(\mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{S}\), which is an element of (M). It is therefore itself an element of (M) by \((\mathrm{e}_{\mathscr{T}})\).

The equivalence between (i) and (iv) generalizes:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.1.7

Under the same hypotheses on \(\mathscr{C}\) and (M), let H be an S-group and X an S-object on which H acts (on the right). Suppose the structural morphism \(\mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{S}\) is an element of (M). The following conditions are equivalent:

(i) H acts freely on X and in an (M)-effective manner.

(ii) There exists an S-morphism \(p : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{Y}\) compatible with the equivalence relation defined in X by the action of H and such that the action of \(\mathrm{H} \times_S \mathrm{Y}\) on X over Y deduced from it makes X a principal homogeneous bundle under \(\mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{Y}}\) over Y.

Under these conditions \(p\) identifies Y with the quotient X/H.

If \(p : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{Y}\) is a morphism compatible with the action of H, then the action of \(\mathrm{H} \times_S \mathrm{Y}\) on X over Y deduced from it defines in X the same equivalence relation as the action of H, by virtue of the formula \[ \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{Y}} \underset{Y}{\times} \mathrm{X} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{H} \underset{S}{\times} \mathrm{X}. \] The proposition follows from this remark and from the preceding equivalence (iv) \(\Leftrightarrow\) (i).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.1.7.1

Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be a category having a final object, stable under fiber products, and equipped with a topology \(\mathscr{T}\) coarser than the canonical topology. Let \(f : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{H}\) be a morphism of \(\mathscr{C}\)-groups, and \(\mathrm{K} = \mathrm{Ker}(f)\). We suppose \(f\) covering for the topology \(\mathscr{T}\).

Then H represents the quotient sheaf G/K, and \(f\) is a \(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{H}}\)-torsor. (N.B. We shall also say that: "G is a K-torsor over H".)

Indeed, since \(f\) is covering, it is a universal effective epimorphism (4.4.3), hence by 3.3.3, H is the quotient of G by the equivalence relation \(\mathscr{R}(f) = \mathrm{G} \times_H \mathrm{G}\). On the other hand, the morphism \(\mathrm{G} \times \mathrm{K} \to \mathrm{G} \times_H \mathrm{G}\), \((g,k) \mapsto (g,gk)\) is an isomorphism of objects with group of operators \(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{G}} = \mathrm{G} \times_H \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{H}}\) (its inverse being given by \((g,g') \mapsto (g,g^{-1}g')\)). Hence, on the one hand, \(\mathscr{R}(f)\) is the equivalence relation defined by K; on the other hand, since the morphism \(f : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{H}\) is covering, \(f\) is a \(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{H}}\)-torsor, by 5.1.6 (ii) (or directly by definition 5.1.5 (ii)).

We can now make theorem 4.6.6 more precise in the case of passage to the quotient by a group of operators:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.1.8

Under the hypotheses of 5.1.7, denote by \(F_0\) the functor over S defined as follows: for each \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\), \(F_0(S')\) is the set of representable sub-S'-functors Z of \(\mathrm{X} \times_S \mathrm{S}'\), stable under \(\mathrm{H} \times_S \mathrm{S}'\) and principal homogeneous bundles under this S'-group for the induced action (3.2.2).

(i) The following conditions are equivalent:

a) The action of H in X is (M)-effective and free.

b) \(F_0\) is representable.

Under these conditions, we have \(F_0 = X/H\).

(ii) Let (N) be a family of morphisms, stable under base change, such that for every covering family \(\{\mathrm{S}_i' \to \mathrm{S}'\}\) and every family \(\{\mathrm{T}_i \to \mathrm{S}_i'\}\) of morphisms of (N), every descent datum on the \(\mathrm{T}_i\) relative to \(\{\mathrm{S}_i' \to \mathrm{S}'\}\) is effective. Suppose the morphism \(\mathrm{X} \times_S \mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{X} \times_S \mathrm{X}\) is an element of (N) and X squarable. Then the element \(p\) of \(\mathrm{Hom}(X,F_0)\) corresponding to the subobject \(\mathrm{X} \times_S \mathrm{H}\) of \(\mathrm{X} \times_S \mathrm{X}\) identifies \(F_0\) with the quotient sheaf X/H.
5.2. Group structures and passage to the quotient

In this section we are interested in the algebraic structures one can put on the quotient G/H of a group by a subgroup. We shall first place ourselves in the category of sheaves on \(\mathscr{C}\) for an arbitrary topology. By taking the canonical topology and using 4.5.12, we shall obtain results for universal effective passage to the quotient in \(\mathscr{C}\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.2.1

Let \(u : \mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{G}\) be a monomorphism of sheaves of groups. There exists on the sheaf G/H a unique structure of object with group of operators G such that the canonical morphism \[ p : G \longrightarrow G/H \] is a morphism of objects with group of operators G. This structure is functorial with respect to the pair (G,H): if we have a commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ H \ar[r] \ar[d] & G \ar[d]^-{f} \\ H' \ar[r] & G' } \] the morphism \(\bar{f} : \mathrm{G}/\mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{G}'/\mathrm{H}'\) (3.2.3) is compatible with the morphism \(f\) on the groups of operators.

Indeed, the sheaf G/H is the sheaf associated with the presheaf \[ i(G)/i(H) : S \longmapsto G(S)/H(S); \] since the functor \(a\) is left exact, it takes objects with groups of operators to objects with group of operators. The presheaf \(i(G)/i(H)\) being equipped with a structure of object with group of operators \(i(G)\), \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{H} = a(i(\mathrm{G})/i(\mathrm{H}))\) is equipped with a structure of object with operators \(a(i(\mathrm{G})) = \mathrm{G}\). This structure evidently enjoys all the stated properties.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.2.2

Let \(u : \mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{G}\) be a monomorphism of \(\mathscr{C}\)-groups. Suppose that the action of H on G is universal effective. There exists on the quotient G/H a unique structure of object with group of operators G such that the morphism \(p : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{G}/\mathrm{H}\) is a morphism of objects with operators. This structure is functorial in the pair (H,G) (H acting in a universal effective manner in G), in the preceding sense.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.2.3

Let \(u : \mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{G}\) be a monomorphism of \(\mathscr{C}\)-groups identifying H with a normal subsheaf of groups of G. There exists on the quotient G/H a unique structure of sheaf of groups such that the canonical morphism \(p : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{G}/\mathrm{H}\) is a group morphism. This structure is functorial in the pair (H,G) (H normal).

The proof is similar to that of 5.2.1.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.2.4

Let \(u : \mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{G}\) be a monomorphism of \(\mathscr{C}\)-groups identifying H with a normal subgroup of G. Suppose that the action of H on G is universal effective. There exists on the quotient G/H a unique group structure such that the canonical morphism \(\mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{G}/\mathrm{H}\) is a group morphism. This structure is functorial with respect to the pair (H,G) (H normal, H acting in a universal effective manner).
One can characterize the group structure of G/H in a more telling way:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.2.5

Under the conditions of 5.2.4, let K be a \(\mathscr{C}\)-group and \(f : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{K}\) a morphism. The following conditions are equivalent:

(i) \(f\) is a group morphism compatible with the equivalence relation defined by H.

(ii) \(f\) is a group morphism inducing the trivial morphism \(\mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{K}\).

(iii) \(f\) factors through a group morphism \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{K}\).

In particular, we have an isomorphism, functorial in the group K, \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{C}\text{-gr.}}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{H},\mathrm{K}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \{f \in \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{C}\text{-gr.}}(\mathrm{G},\mathrm{K}),\ f\circ u = e\}. \]

The equivalence of (i) and (ii) is proved set-theoretically. We evidently have (iii) \(\Rightarrow\) (ii). The equivalence of (iii) and (ii) follows from the formula \(\mathrm{Hom}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{H},\mathrm{K}) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}(i(\mathrm{G})/i(\mathrm{H}),\mathrm{K})\) and from the definition of the group structure of G/H.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 5.2.6

In the preceding situation, if the kernel of \(f\) is exactly H, the morphism \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{K}\) factoring \(f\) is a monomorphism. This follows at once from 3.3.4.

In the case of sheaves of groups, we can make 4.4.11 more precise by the following

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.2.7

Let G be a sheaf of groups and H a normal subsheaf of groups. For every subsheaf of groups K of G containing H, let K' be the quotient group K/H regarded as a subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}' = \mathrm{G}/\mathrm{H}\). We have \(\mathrm{K} = \mathrm{K}' \times_{G'} \mathrm{G}\). The maps \(\mathrm{K} \mapsto \mathrm{K}/\mathrm{H}\) and \(\mathrm{K}' \mapsto \mathrm{K}' \times_{G'} \mathrm{G}\) realize a bijective correspondence between the set of subsheaves of groups of G containing H and the set of subsheaves of groups of G'. In this correspondence, the normal subsheaves of groups of G correspond to the normal subsheaves of G'.

The first part follows easily from 4.4.11 and 3.2.4. It remains to see that K is normal in G if and only if K' is normal in G'. If K is normal in G, then the presheaf \(i(\mathrm{K})/i(\mathrm{H})\) is normal in \(i(\mathrm{G})/i(\mathrm{H})\). The same holds for the associated sheaves, by virtue of the usual argument. If conversely K' is normal in G', then the fiber product \(\mathrm{K} \times_{G'} \mathrm{G}\) is normal in G, as one sees immediately.

If now L is an arbitrary subsheaf of groups of G, let \(\bar{L}\) be the saturation of L for the equivalence relation defined by H; we shall also write \(\bar{\mathrm{L}} = \mathrm{L}\cdot\mathrm{H}\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.2.8

Under the preceding conditions, \(\mathrm{L}\cdot\mathrm{H}\) is a subsheaf of groups of G containing H and the image of L in G/H is identified with \[ (\mathrm{L}\cdot\mathrm{H})/\mathrm{H} \simeq \mathrm{L}/(\mathrm{H}\cap\mathrm{L}). \]

Indeed, denote by L' the image sheaf of L in G/H. It is a subsheaf of groups of G/H corresponding to \(\mathrm{L}\cdot\mathrm{H}\) in the correspondence of the preceding proposition. Since the morphism \(\mathrm{L} \to \mathrm{L}'\) is covering, hence a universal effective epimorphism of sheaves, it follows from 4.4.9 that L' is identified with the quotient of L by the kernel of \(\mathrm{L} \to \mathrm{L}'\), which is evidently none other than \(\mathrm{H}\cap\mathrm{L}\).

Consider finally the following situation: we have a sheaf of groups G, a subsheaf of groups K and a subsheaf of groups H of K, normal in K. Let us first define a (right) action of the sheaf of groups \(\mathrm{H}\backslash\mathrm{K}\) (\(=\mathrm{K}/\mathrm{H}\)) on G/H. The group K acts by right translations on G. Since H is normal in K, this action is compatible with the equivalence relation defined by the action of H and therefore defines an action of K on G/H, that is, a morphism from the group \(\mathrm{K}^{\circ}\) opposite to K to \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{H})\). Since the latter is a sheaf (4.5.13) and this morphism is trivial on H, it factors through K/H and defines the desired action. Since the actions of G on itself on the right and on the left commute, the actions of G and of K/H on G/H commute.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.2.9

Under the preceding conditions, K/H acts freely (on the right) in G/H and we have a canonical isomorphism of sheaves with group of operators G \[ (\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{H})/(\mathrm{K}/\mathrm{H}) \simeq \mathrm{G}/\mathrm{K}. \]

When K is normal in G, in which case K/H is normal in G/H (5.2.7), this isomorphism respects the group structures of the two sides.

We have an isomorphism of presheaves \[ \bigl(i(\mathrm{G})/i(\mathrm{H})\bigr)\big/\bigl(i(\mathrm{K})/i(\mathrm{H})\bigr) \xleftarrow{\ \sim\ } i(\mathrm{G})/i(\mathrm{K}), \] which respects the structures of objects with group of operators \(i(G)\). The announced result is obtained by applying the functor \(a\) to this relation.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.2.10

Let G be a \(\mathscr{C}\)-group, K a sub-\(\mathscr{C}\)-group of G, H a normal sub-\(\mathscr{C}\)-group of K. Let (M) be a family of morphisms of \(\mathscr{C}\) satisfying axioms (a) to \((\mathrm{e}_{\mathscr{T}})\). Suppose the right action of H on G (resp. K) is (M)-effective. Then K/H acts in a natural way freely on the right on G/H; this action commutes with that of G. The following conditions are equivalent:

(i) The action of K on G is (M)-effective.

(ii) The action of K/H on G/H is (M)-effective.

Under these conditions, we have an isomorphism of objects with group of operators G: \[ (\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{H})/(\mathrm{K}/\mathrm{H}) \simeq \mathrm{G}/\mathrm{K}. \]
5.3. Use of effectivity criteria: Noether theorem
Let \(\mathscr{C}\), \(\mathscr{T}\) and (M) be as usual. Let (N) be a family of morphisms satisfying axioms (a) and \((\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{M}})\) of 4.7.
Putting together 5.2.7 and 4.7.2, we obtain:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.3.1

Let G be a \(\mathscr{C}\)-group. Let H be a sub-\(\mathscr{C}\)-group of G, normal and acting in an (M)-effective manner in G. For every sub-\(\mathscr{C}\)-group K of G dominating H and such that the morphism \(\mathrm{K} \to \mathrm{G}\) is an element of (N), H acts in K in an (M)-effective manner and the quotient \(\mathrm{K}/\mathrm{H} = \mathrm{K}'\) is a sub-\(\mathscr{C}\)-group of \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{H} = \mathrm{G}'\) such that the morphism \(\mathrm{K}' \to \mathrm{G}'\) is an element of (N). The map \(\mathrm{K} \mapsto \mathrm{K}'\) realizes a bijective correspondence between the set of sub-\(\mathscr{C}\)-groups K of G, dominating H and such that \(\mathrm{K} \to \mathrm{G}\) is an element of (N), and the set of sub-\(\mathscr{C}\)-groups K' of G' such that \(\mathrm{K}' \to \mathrm{G}'\) is an element of (N). The inverse map is \(\mathrm{K}' \mapsto \mathrm{K} \times_{G'} \mathrm{G}\). In this correspondence, the normal subgroups of G correspond to the normal subgroups of G'.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.3.2

If \(\mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{G}\) is an element of (N), then \(\mathscr{C}\) has a final object \(e\) and the unit section \(e \to \mathrm{G}/\mathrm{H}\) is an element of (N).

pages.title: 6. Topologies in the category of schemes

blocks.html: 6. Topologies in the category of schemes
6.1. The Zariski topology.

This is the topology generated by the following pretopology: a family of morphisms \(\{S_i \to S\}\) is covering if each morphism is an open immersion and if the union of the images of the \(S_i\) is all of S. It is denoted (Zar).

A sheaf for the Zariski topology is also called a functor of local nature: it is a contravariant functor from (Sch) to (Ens) such that for every scheme S and every covering of S by open sets \(S_i\), we have an exact diagram: \[ \mathrm{F}(S) \longrightarrow \prod_i \mathrm{F}(S_i) \rightrightarrows \prod_{i,j} \mathrm{F}(S_i \cap S_j). \]
In particular, a functor of local nature takes direct sums to products. Since every representable functor is a sheaf, this topology is coarser than the canonical topology.
As regards terminology, whenever we say "local" or "locally" without further specification, it will be with reference to the Zariski topology, hence in the usual sense.
6.2. A procedure for constructing topologies

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6.2.1

Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be a category and \(\mathscr{C}'\) a full subcategory. Let P be a set of families of morphisms of \(\mathscr{C}\) with the same target, stable under base change and under composition (that is, satisfying axioms (P 1) and (P 2) of 4.2.5). Let \(P'\) be a set of morphisms of \(\mathscr{C}'\). We assume that \(P'\) contains the families reduced to an identity isomorphism (P 3) and satisfies the following condition:

  1. (a)

    If \(\{S_i \to S\} \in P'\) (so \(S_i, S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}'\)) and if \(T \to S\) is a morphism of \(\mathscr{C}'\), then the fiber products \(S_i \times_S T\) (in \(\mathscr{C}\)) exist and the family \(\{S_i \times_S T \to T\} \in P'\) (so \(S_i \times_S T \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}'\)). (Remark: this condition implies that \(P'\) is stable under base change in \(\mathscr{C}'\), but is not equivalent to it, for it assumes in addition that the inclusion functor from \(\mathscr{C}'\) to \(\mathscr{C}\) commutes with certain fiber products).

We assume moreover that the two conditions below are satisfied:

  1. (b)

    For every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\), there exists \(\{S_i \to S\} \in P\) with \(S_i \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}'\) for each i.

  2. (c)

    In the following situation: \[ \xymatrix{ & S_{ijk} \ar[d]^-{(\mathrm{P}')} \\ S_i \ar[d]_-{(\mathrm{P}')} & S_{ij} \ar[l]_-{(\mathrm{P})} \\ S & } \] where \(S, S_i, S_{ij}, S_{ijk} \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}'\); \(\{S_i \to S\} \in P'\); \(\{S_{ij} \to S_i\} \in P\) for each i; \(\{S_{ijk} \to S_{ij}\} \in P'\) for each ij, there exist a family \(\{T_n \to S\} \in P'\) and for each n a multi-index \((ijk)\) and a commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ S_{ijk} \ar[d] & T_n \ar[l] \ar[dl] \\ S & } \]

Equip \(\mathscr{C}\) with the topology generated by P and \(P'\). Let \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\) and R a sieve of S. For R to be covering, it is necessary and sufficient that there exist a composite family \(\{S_{ij} \to S_i \to S\}\), where \(S_i, S_{ij} \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}'\), \(\{S_i \to S\} \in P\), \(\{S_{ij} \to S_i\} \in P'\) for each i, such that the resulting morphisms \(S_{ij} \to S\) factor through R (in other words, that the sieve generated by this composite family be contained in R).

Proof. The families belonging to P and \(P'\) being covering, a family composed of such families will be covering as well (C 2), hence a sieve of the indicated form is covering, since it contains a covering sieve. Conversely, it suffices to see that the sieves of this form do indeed form a topology, that is, to check axioms (T 1) to (T 4) of 4.2.1.

Axiom (T 4). Let \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\). By (b) there exists a family \(\{S_i \to S\} \in P\) with \(S_i \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}'\). The families \(\{S_i \xrightarrow{\mathrm{id}_{S_i}} S_i\}\) belong to \(P'\) by hypothesis. The sieve S of S is therefore of the desired form: \[ \xymatrix{ S_i \ar[r] \ar[d]_-{\mathrm{id}}^{(\mathrm{P}')} & S \ar[d]^-{\mathrm{id}_S} \\ S_i \ar[r]_-{(\mathrm{P})} & S } \]
Axiom (T 3). Obvious.

Axiom (T 2). Let R be a sieve of S of the desired form and let C be an arbitrary sieve of S. Since \(S_{ij} \to S\) factors through R, the sieve \(C_{ij} = C \times_S S_{ij}\) of \(S_{ij}\) is of the desired form by hypothesis. \[ \xymatrix{ S_{ij} \ar[dr] & & C_{ij} \ar@{_{(}->}[ll] \ar[dd] \\ & S_i \ar[d] & \\ R \ar@{_{(}->}[r] & S & C \ar@{_{(}->}[l] } \] We thus have for each ij a diagram of the form opposite. We have therefore proved that there exists a composite family \[ S_{ijkl} \xrightarrow{(\mathrm{P}')} S_{ijk} \xrightarrow{(\mathrm{P})} S_{ij} \xrightarrow{(\mathrm{P}')} S_i \xrightarrow{(\mathrm{P})} S \] belonging to \(P \circ P' \circ P \circ P'\), factoring through C, and in which all the objects other than S are in \(\mathscr{C}'\). \[ \xymatrix{ S_{ijkl} \ar[d] \ar[ddr] & \\ S_{ijk} \ar[d] & \\ S_{ij} & C_{ij} \ar@{_{(}->}[l] } \] Applying condition (c) to each family \(\{S_{ijkl} \to S_i\}\), we deduce that for each i there exists a family \(\{T_{in} \to S_i\} \in P'\) such that \(T_{in} \to S\) factors through one of the \(S_{ijkl}\), hence through C: \[ \xymatrix{ T_{in} \ar[r] \ar[d]_-{(\mathrm{P}')} & S_{ijkl} \ar[dd] \\ S_i \ar[d]_-{(\mathrm{P})} & \\ S & C \ar@{_{(}->}[l] } \] The sieve C is therefore of the desired form, which completes the verification.

Axiom (T 1). Let R be a sieve of S of the given form and let \(T \to S\) be a morphism of \(\mathscr{C}\). \[ \xymatrix{ & & S_{ij} \ar[dl] & U_{ikj} \ar[l] \ar[d] \\ & S_i \ar[dl] & T_i \ar[l] & U_{ik} \ar[l] \\ R \ar@{_{(}->}[r] & S & T \ar[l] & } \qquad \xymatrix{ U_{ikj} \ar[r] \ar[d] & R \ar@{_{(}->}[d] \\ T \ar[r] & S } \] Let \(T_i = S_i \times_S T\). The family \(\{T_i \to T\}\) belongs to P (by (P 1)). Applying (b), we construct families \(\{U_{ik} \to T_i\} \in P\), with the \(U_{ik} \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}'\). By hypothesis (condition (P 2) on P), we have \(\{U_{ik} \to T\} \in P\). By (a), \(U_{ik} \times_{S_i} S_{ij} = U_{ikj}\) is an object of \(\mathscr{C}'\) and for each ik, \(\{U_{ikj} \to U_{ik}\} \in P'\). The commutative square above then shows that the morphisms \(U_{ijk} \to T\) factor through the sieve \(T \times_S R\) of T, which is therefore of the desired form, which completes the proof.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.2.2

If \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}'\) and if R is a sieve of S, R is covering if and only if there exists a family \(\{T_i \to S\} \in P'\) factoring through R.

Indeed, such a sieve is covering. On the other hand it suffices to apply (c), taking the family \(\{S_i \to S\}\) reduced to the identity isomorphism of S, to deduce from the proposition that a covering sieve is of the indicated form.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.2.3

For a presheaf F on \(\mathscr{C}\) to be separated (resp. a sheaf), it is necessary and sufficient that the morphism \[ \mathrm{F}(S) \longrightarrow \prod_i \mathrm{F}(S_i) \] be injective (resp. the diagram \[ \mathrm{F}(S) \longrightarrow \prod_i \mathrm{F}(S_i) \rightrightarrows \prod_{i,j} \mathrm{F}(S_i \times_S S_j) \] exact) in the two following cases:

  1. (i)

    \(\{S_i \to S\} \in P\),

  2. (ii)

    \(S, S_i \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}'\); \(\{S_i \to S\} \in P'\).

Indeed, the conditions are necessary, since the families in question are covering. If C is a sieve of S generated by a family of morphisms of the indicated type, an easy diagram chase shows that the conditions of the corollary imply that \(\mathrm{Hom}(S,\mathrm{F}) \xrightarrow{\ g\ } \mathrm{Hom}(C,\mathrm{F})\) is injective (resp. bijective). But every refinement R of S contains a sieve C of the above type and we have a commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{Hom}(S,\mathrm{F}) \ar[rr]^-{f} \ar[dr]_-{g} & & \mathrm{Hom}(R,\mathrm{F}) \ar[dl]^-{h} \\ & \mathrm{Hom}(C,\mathrm{F}) & } \] We know that g is injective, hence so is f. Hence F is separated. But R is a refinement of C, so h is also injective. If g is bijective, then f is as well, so F is a sheaf.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 6.2.4

The preceding corollary does not follow from 4.3.5, since \(P'\) is not stable under base extension.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 6.2.5

Condition (c) is satisfied in particular in the case where

  1. (i)

    \(P'\) is stable under composition.

  2. (ii)

    If \(\{S_i \to S\}\) is a family of morphisms of \(\mathscr{C}'\) belonging to P, there exists a subfamily of it belonging to \(P'\).
6.3. Application to the category of schemes.

We take for \(\mathscr{C}\) the category of schemes, for \(\mathscr{C}'\) the full subcategory consisting of affine schemes, for P the set of surjective families of open immersions. We shall consider several sets \(P'\):
\(P'_1\): finite surjective families, composed of flat morphisms.
\(P'_2\): finite surjective families, composed of flat quasi-finite morphisms of finite presentation.
\(P'_3\): finite surjective families, composed of étale morphisms.
\(P'_4\): finite surjective families, composed of finite étale morphisms.

For each of these sets \(P'_i\), except \(P'_4\), the conditions of the proposition are satisfied ((c) thanks to 6.2.5, since an affine scheme being quasi-compact, every family of morphisms of \(\mathscr{C}'\) belonging to P contains a finite subfamily which is also in P, hence in \(P'_i\)). The topology \(\mathscr{T}_i\) generated by P and \(P'_i\) is denoted and named as follows:

\(\mathscr{T}_1\)\(=\)(fpqc)\(=\)faithfully flat quasi-compact topology.
\(\mathscr{T}_2\)\(=\)(fppf)\(=\)faithfully flat (locally) of finite presentation topology.
\(\mathscr{T}_3\)\(=\)(ét)\(=\)étale topology.
\(\mathscr{T}_4\)\(=\)(étf)\(=\)finite étale topology.
Since \(P'_1 \supset P'_2 \supset P'_3 \supset P'_4\), we have \[ \text{(fpqc)} \geqslant \text{(fppf)} \geqslant \text{(ét)} \geqslant \text{(étf)} \geqslant \text{(Zar)}. \]

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6.3.1
  1. (i)

    For the sieve R of S to be covering for \(\mathscr{T}_i\), \(1 \leqslant i \leqslant 3\), it is necessary and sufficient that there exist a covering \((S_p)\) of S by affine open sets and for each p a family \(\{S_{pq} \to S_p\}\) belonging to \(P'_i\), the \(S_{pq}\) being affine, such that each morphism \(\{S_{pq} \to S\}\) factors through R.

  2. (ii)

    For a presheaf F on (Sch) to be a sheaf for (fpqc) (resp. (fppf), (ét), (étf)), it is necessary and sufficient that

    1. a)

      F be a sheaf for (Zar), i.e. a functor of local nature.

    2. b)

      For every faithfully flat (resp. faithfully flat quasi-finite of finite presentation, resp. surjective étale, resp. surjective finite étale) morphism \(T \to S\), where T and S are affine, we have an exact diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{F}(S) \ar[r] & \mathrm{F}(T) \ar@<0.6ex>[r] \ar@<-0.6ex>[r] & \mathrm{F}(T\underset{S}{\times}T) } \]

  3. (iii)

    The topologies \(\mathscr{T}_i\), \(i = 1,2,3,4\), are coarser than the canonical topology.

  4. (iv)

    Every surjective family consisting of flat open morphisms (resp. flat and locally of finite presentation, resp. étale, resp. finite étale) is covering for (fpqc) (resp. (fppf) resp. (fpqf), resp. (ét), resp. (étf)).

  5. (v)

    Every finite surjective family consisting of flat quasi-compact morphisms is covering for (fpqc). In particular, every faithfully flat quasi-compact morphism is covering for (fpqc).

Proof. (i) follows from 6.2.1, (ii) from 6.2.3, taking into account the fact that a sheaf for the Zariski topology takes direct sums to products. Every representable functor being a sheaf for (Zar) and satisfying condition (b) of (ii) by SGA 1, VIII 5.3, \(\mathscr{T}_1\) is coarser than the canonical topology, which proves (iii).

Let us prove (iv). Let \(\{S_i \to S\}\) be a family of morphisms as in the statement. Considering a covering of S by affine open sets, we reduce immediately to the case where S is affine. Then let \(S_{ij}\) be a covering of \(S_i\) by affine open sets such that the restriction of \(S_i \to S\) to \(S_{ij}\) is arbitrary (resp. of finite presentation and quasi-finite EGA IV, resp. arbitrary, resp. arbitrary). Since the morphisms in question are open, the images \(T_{ij}\) of the \(S_{ij}\) form an open covering of S. Let \(T_{ijk}\) be an affine open covering of \(T_{ij}\). The \(S_{ijk} = S_{ij} \times_S T_{ijk}\) are affine, \[ \xymatrix{ & S_{ij} \ar[dl] \ar[d] & S_{ijk} \ar[l] \ar[d] \\ S_i \ar[d] & T_{ij} & T_{ijk} \ar[l] \\ S & & } \] and the morphisms \(S_{ijk} \to T_{ijk}\) are surjective, flat and open (resp. flat quasi-finite of finite presentation, resp. étale, resp. finite étale). The composite family \(\{S_{ijk} \to T_{ijk} \to S\}\) is therefore covering by (i). Since it factors through the given family, the latter is covering.

Let us prove (v). Let \(\{S_i \to S\}\) be a finite faithfully flat quasi-compact family. Let \(T_j\) be a covering of S by affine open sets. The \(S_{ij} = T_j \times_S S_i\) are quasi-compact and therefore have finite affine open coverings \(T_{ijk}\). Each morphism \(T_{ijk} \to T_j\) is flat, and the family \(\{T_{ijk} \to T_j\}\) is finite and surjective. The family \(\{T_{ijk} \to S\}\) is covering by (i). It factors through the given family, which is therefore covering as well: \[ \xymatrix{ S_i \ar[d] & S_{ij} \ar[l] \ar[d] & T_{ijk} \ar[l] \\ S & T_j \ar[l] & } \]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.3.2

Let \((M_i)\) be the following family of morphisms:

\((M_1)\): faithfully flat quasi-compact morphisms.

\((M_2)\): faithfully flat morphisms locally of finite presentation.

\((M_3)\): étale morphisms.

\((M_4)\): finite étale morphisms.

The family \((M_i)\) satisfies axioms (a), (b), (c), \((\mathrm{d}_{\mathscr{T}_i})\) and \((\mathrm{e}_{\mathscr{T}_i})\) of 4.6.3.

Indeed, for (a), (b), (c), this is classical (EGA and SGA 1, passim.). By 6.3.1, (iv) and (v), \((M_i)\) satisfies \((\mathrm{d}_{\mathscr{T}_i})\). It remains to see that \((M_i)\) satisfies \((\mathrm{e}_{\mathscr{T}_i})\); for this, it suffices to see that \((M_i)\) satisfies \((\mathrm{e}_{\mathscr{T}_1})\), which implies the others. This follows from SGA 1, VIII (nos. 4 and 5).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.3.3

If X is a scheme and R an equivalence relation in X of type \((M_i)\), R is \((M_i)\)-effective if and only if the quotient sheaf of X by R for \(\mathscr{T}_i\) is representable, and in this case it is represented by the quotient \(\mathrm{X}/\mathrm{R}\).
Indeed, this is 4.6.5.
6.4. Effectivity conditions.

We now look for families (N) of morphisms satisfying axiom \((\mathrm{f}_{\mathscr{T}})\) of 4.7. Note first that \((\mathrm{f}_{\mathscr{T}_1})\) implies \((\mathrm{f}_{\mathscr{T}_i})\), so that we may restrict ourselves to the case of the topology (fpqc).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.4.1

The following families of morphisms satisfy axiom \((\mathrm{f}_{\mathscr{T}_1})\) of 4.7, that is, "descend by (fpqc)":

(N): open immersions.

\((\mathrm{N}')\): closed immersions.

\((\mathrm{N}'')\): quasi-compact immersions.

By virtue of 6.3.1 (ii), it suffices to check that the given families descend by the Zariski topology and by a faithfully flat quasi-compact morphism. The first assertion is clear; let us check the second. For (N), it is SGA 1, VIII 4.4, for \((\mathrm{N}')\), it is loc. cit., 1.9. For \((\mathrm{N}'')\) one argues as in loc. cit., 5.5 using the two earlier results.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.4.2

The same result holds for quasi-compact open immersions.
These results allow us to apply to the present situation the general results of 4.7.1, 4.7.2, 5.1.8, 5.3.1, etc... Let us state one of them as an example, the first.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.4.3

(\(=\) 4.7.1 \(+\) 4.6.10). Let X be a scheme and R an equivalence relation in X. We suppose that \(\mathrm{R} \to \mathrm{X}\) is faithfully flat and quasi-compact and that \(\mathrm{R} \to \mathrm{X} \times \mathrm{X}\) is a closed (resp. open, resp. quasi-compact, resp. quasi-compact open) immersion. Then the quotient sheaf \(\mathrm{X}/\mathrm{R}\) is the same for the topology (fpqc) and for the canonical topology, and for each scheme S, we have

\(\mathrm{X}/\mathrm{R}\,(S)\) = set of closed (resp. open, retrocompact, resp. retrocompact open) subschemes \(Z\) of \(\mathrm{X}_S\), stable under \(\mathrm{R}_S\), such that \(Z \to S\) is faithfully flat quasi-compact and the diagram \(\mathrm{R}_Z \rightrightarrows Z \to S\) exact.
6.5. Principal homogeneous bundles.
Let us simply record the terminology:

topologyterminology
(fpqc)(plain)
(ét)quasi-isotrivial
(étf)locally isotrivial
(Zar)locally trivial.
6.6. Other topologies.

Other topologies on the category of schemes are sometimes used. Let us mention one: the global finite étale topology (étfg), generated by the pretopology whose covering families are the surjective families consisting of finite étale morphisms. It is not finer than the Zariski topology. The corresponding principal homogeneous bundles are called "isotrivial".

\[ \xymatrix{ & (\text{canonical}) \ar[d] & \\ & (\text{fpqc}) \ar[d] & \\ & (\text{fppf}) \ar[d] & \\ & (\text{ét}) \ar[d] & \\ & (\text{étf}) \ar[dl] \ar[dr] & \\ (\text{Zar}) \ar[dr] & & (\text{étfg}) \ar[dl] \\ & (\text{chaotic}) & } \]
